payload.description:
Add graph expansion as a third candidate source alongside FTS and vector search in the hybrid ranking engine. When a query returns top-k candidates from vector+FTS, expand their immediate wiki-link neighbors (1 hop) and add those as additional candidates before fusion scoring.

Implementation approach:
1. After the initial vector (top-50) and FTS (top-50) candidate retrieval, take the top-N highest-scored seed candidates (e.g., top-10 after initial fusion).
2. For each seed, query SQLite links table for 1-hop neighbors: SELECT dst_chunk_id FROM links WHERE src_chunk_id IN (...) UNION SELECT src_chunk_id FROM links WHERE dst_chunk_id IN (...).
3. Add neighbor chunks to the candidate pool (deduplicate by chunk_id against existing candidates).
4. Score all candidates (including graph-expanded ones) through the existing hybrid formula.
5. Optionally add a graph_proximity signal to the hybrid formula: chunks discovered via graph expansion get a proximity boost based on the seed's score.

Configurable parameters:
- graph_expansion_enabled: bool (default true)
- graph_expansion_hops: u8 (default 1, max 2)
- graph_expansion_seed_k: usize (default 10 — only expand neighbors of top-10 seeds)
- graph_expansion_max_candidates: usize (default 100 — cap total graph-expanded candidates to prevent blowup)

Why this matters: In a wiki-linked vault, a query about 'deployment decisions' might match a decision log that links to [[kubernetes-setup]] and [[ci-pipeline]]. Without graph expansion, those linked notes must independently match the query. With 1-hop expansion, transitively relevant content is captured.

Acceptance: (1) A query that matches note A, where A links to note B, returns B in results even if B has low direct vector/FTS similarity. (2) Graph expansion respects the max_candidates cap. (3) Performance overhead is bounded (1-hop expansion adds at most one batch SQLite query).

## Notes

Phase ownership: This is a Phase 2 Polish item (depends on brain-e69). The graph expansion work feeds into Phase 3 performance tuning but is logically Phase 2 scope.
payload.title: Implement 1-hop graph expansion as third retrieval candidate source

payload.description: Goal: 'I can point at some markdown files and immediately retrieve them semantically.' Prove the fundamental pipeline works: directory scan -> minimal chunking -> local BGE embedding via Candle -> LanceDB vector store -> semantic query with top-k retrieval. No SQLite, no watcher, no MCP — just the raw loop. For the POC, a note directory path is passed as a CLI arg (the brain registry model comes later in Phase 4). Success criteria: you can run index then query and it 'feels right'.
payload.title: Phase 0 — POC: Prove the Core Loop End-to-End

payload.description:
Set up the initial Cargo.toml with only the dependencies needed for the POC phase. This means: tokio (rt-multi-thread, macros, fs), anyhow for error handling, thiserror for typed errors, tracing + tracing-subscriber (with env-filter feature) for structured logging, candle-core/candle-nn/candle-transformers (0.9+) for BERT inference, tokenizers (0.22+) for HuggingFace tokenizer loading, lancedb (0.26+) for vector storage, arrow-schema + arrow-array (let lancedb drive the arrow version — do NOT independently specify arrow versions to avoid conflicts), and walkdir for directory traversal. Use Rust edition 2021 (not 2024 — the 2024 edition is not yet stable as of early 2026). Pin compatible versions carefully — arrow and lancedb versions must align. Do NOT add SQLite, notify, blake3, or uuid yet; those come in Phase 1. IMPORTANT: Let LanceDB's Cargo.toml drive the Arrow crate version. Specify lancedb first, then use the same arrow version it pulls in. Acceptance: cargo check succeeds with all deps resolved, no version conflicts.

## Notes

KNOWN BUG: The existing Cargo.toml has edition = "2024" which must be changed to edition = "2021" as part of this task. Also: resolve whether to include clap as a dependency for brain-0de.9 (CLI). The CLI task mentions 'clap-style argument parsing or manual std::env::args' — decide here and include clap if chosen.
payload.title: Configure Cargo.toml with core POC dependencies

payload.description:
Create a minimal test suite that validates the POC pipeline end-to-end. Tests: (1) Embedder test: load model, embed a known sentence, verify output shape is (1, 384) and L2 norm ≈ 1.0. (2) Embedder consistency test: same input text produces identical embeddings across calls. (3) Chunker test: known markdown content produces expected number of non-empty chunks. (4) Round-trip test: create a temp directory with 2-3 .md files, run the full index pipeline, then query — verify the most relevant file appears in top results. (5) Empty vault test: indexing an empty directory doesn't crash. Use a test fixture directory with sample .md files checked into the repo under tests/fixtures/. Acceptance: all tests pass; the round-trip test demonstrates semantic relevance (not just 'doesn't crash'). NOTE: This ticket validates the POC only — it does NOT depend on Phase 1. All tests should use the POC CLI and POC infrastructure.

## Notes

DESIGN NOTE: Consider refactoring Embedder to use a trait (e.g. trait Embed { fn embed_batch(&self, texts: &[&str]) -> Result<Vec<Vec<f32>>>; fn hidden_size(&self) -> usize; }) before writing these tests. This would allow injecting a mock embedder that returns fixed vectors, avoiding the need to load a real BERT model in CI. The trait introduction is only worth doing when we actually need the second implementation (the mock), so it pairs naturally with this ticket.
payload.title: Write POC smoke tests and validation

payload.body: QA review findings addressed: (1) tightened loose assertions — heading_splits now exact 6, fixture_headings exact 21, fixture indexed count exact 9; (2) score assertions no longer silently skip — added explicit is_some() guard before unwrap; (3) added test_chunker_fixture_large validating all chunks <= MAX_CHUNK_CHARS; (4) documented semantic relevance limitation in module doc — MockEmbedder validates plumbing via identity matching, not true semantic similarity. Golden DB approach deferred to CI setup.

payload.description:
Implement model weight acquisition for the BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 embedding model. The system needs three files locally: config.json, tokenizer.json, and model.safetensors. Implementation options (choose one): (1) Use the hf-hub crate to download from HuggingFace Hub on first run, caching to a configurable model_dir (default: ~/.cache/brain/models/bge-small-en-v1.5). (2) Accept a --model-dir CLI flag pointing to pre-downloaded weights. For the POC, option (2) is sufficient — document the manual download steps (huggingface-cli download BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 --local-dir ./models/bge-small-en-v1.5). Add hf-hub to Cargo.toml as an optional dependency behind a 'download' feature flag for future automatic downloading. Validate on startup: check that all three files exist and are non-empty; print a clear error with download instructions if missing. RESEARCH NEEDED: Verify the exact filenames in the BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 HuggingFace repo (some models split weights across multiple .safetensors shards). Verify hf-hub crate API stability and whether it supports offline/cache-only mode. Acceptance: (1) With pre-downloaded weights, brain-0de.5 model loader can find and load them. (2) Missing weights produce a helpful error message with download instructions. (3) Model directory path is configurable via CLI flag.

## Notes

RESOLVED: Removed hf-hub crate due to Content-Range header bug in v0.5.0 (both ureq and tokio/reqwest backends affected). Model download now handled by scripts/setup-model.sh which uses huggingface-cli (brew install huggingface-cli on macOS, pipx install huggingface-hub on Linux). Embedder::load() now takes a required &Path instead of Option<&Path>. CLI --model-dir defaults to ./models/bge-small-en-v1.5.
payload.title: Download and cache BGE-small-en-v1.5 model weights

payload.description: Define and implement the project-wide error handling and logging conventions before any feature code is written. This is a cross-cutting concern that prevents inconsistent patterns across 35+ tickets. Error handling: (1) Use anyhow::Result as the return type for all binary-level functions (main, CLI handlers). (2) Define a brain::Error enum using thiserror for library-internal errors with variants: IoError, EmbeddingError, StorageError (SQLite), VectorDbError (LanceDB), ParseError, ConfigError. Each variant wraps the underlying error. (3) Partial failure policy for indexing: if a single file fails to parse/embed/store, log a warning with the file path and error, skip that file, and continue processing other files. Do NOT abort the entire batch. Collect and report skipped files at the end. (4) Fatal vs recoverable: model load failure is fatal (abort startup). SQLite open failure is fatal. LanceDB connection failure is fatal. Individual file processing failures are recoverable. Tracing setup: (1) Add tracing (0.1) and tracing-subscriber (0.3) to Cargo.toml. (2) Initialize tracing-subscriber with EnvFilter (RUST_LOG env var) in main.rs. Default level: info. (3) Use #[instrument] on key public functions. (4) Log to stderr (stdout is reserved for MCP JSON-RPC in later phases). (5) Use structured fields: file_path, file_id, chunk_count, duration_ms. Acceptance: (1) cargo check passes with error types and tracing macros. (2) RUST_LOG=debug cargo run produces structured log output on stderr. (3) Error enum covers all planned error categories. (4) Partial failure policy is documented in code comments.
payload.title: Establish error handling strategy and tracing setup

payload.description:
Create a CI configuration (GitHub Actions or equivalent) that runs quality gates automatically. This ensures tests from brain-0de.10 (POC smoke tests) onward are verified on every commit. Steps: (1) Create .github/workflows/ci.yml (or equivalent if not using GitHub). (2) Jobs: cargo check (compilation), cargo test (all tests), cargo clippy -- -D warnings (lint). (3) Cache cargo registry and target directory for speed. (4) Use stable Rust toolchain matching edition 2021. (5) Matrix: test on at least Linux (primary target). macOS optional. (6) Fail the pipeline on any warning from clippy. (7) Include a step to verify cargo fmt --check for consistent formatting. NOTE: Since this project has no git remote configured yet, this task creates the CI config files but cannot activate them until a remote is set up. The files should be ready to go when a remote is added. Acceptance: CI config files exist and are syntactically valid. Running the equivalent commands locally (cargo check, cargo test, cargo clippy, cargo fmt --check) succeeds.

## Notes

COORDINATION NOTE: The Rust toolchain version pinned in mise.toml (brain-0de.15) should match the version used in CI. Ensure consistency.
payload.title: Set up CI pipeline with cargo check, test, and clippy

The current smoke tests use MockEmbedder (hash-based) which validates pipeline plumbing but not semantic query quality. payload.description: Create a tests/fixtures/ directory with representative Markdown files used across all test suites. Multiple test tasks (brain-0de.10, brain-ffh.10, brain-dhi.11) reference fixture files but none creates a shared set, risking duplicate/inconsistent test data. Fixtures needed: (1) simple.md — basic paragraphs, no frontmatter, no headings. (2) headings.md — nested heading hierarchy (h1 through h4) with content under each. (3) frontmatter.md — YAML frontmatter with title, tags, date, aliases, and custom fields. (4) wikilinks.md — contains [[wiki-links]], [[target|display text]], and [markdown](links.md). (5) tasks.md — contains - [ ] and - [x] items with due dates and tags. (6) code_blocks.md — fenced code blocks in multiple languages (should be preserved as atomic chunks). (7) large.md — a longer document (~5000 words) for testing chunk splitting and token budgets. (8) empty.md — empty file (edge case). (9) frontmatter_only.md — only frontmatter, no body content. All fixtures should have realistic content so semantic search tests are meaningful (not lorem ipsum). Acceptance: tests/fixtures/ directory exists with all listed files; files are checked into git; content is realistic and covers the documented edge cases.

payload.description:
Create a mise.toml file at the project root to pin and manage transient dependencies (tools, runtimes, and utilities) that the project needs but are not tracked by Cargo. This ensures reproducible builds and onboarding across machines. Tools to pin: (1) rust — pin the exact stable Rust toolchain version (matching edition 2021 compatibility). (2) huggingface-cli — needed for downloading BGE model weights (see brain-0de.11). (3) Any other CLI tools identified during POC development. Configuration: (1) Use [tools] section to pin versions. (2) Add a [env] section for project-specific environment variables if needed (e.g., RUST_LOG default, model cache directory). (3) Document in a comment block what each tool is for and why it's pinned. Acceptance: (1) mise.toml exists at project root. (2) Running 'mise install' installs all required tools. (3) Rust toolchain version is pinned and matches CI expectations (brain-0de.13).

## Notes

STATUS: mise.toml exists with RUST_LOG=info. HuggingFace model download is handled in-app via the hf-hub crate (no need to pin huggingface-cli). Rust toolchain management is left to the user via rustup (not pinned in mise). REMAINING: Add justfile support for common dev tasks (build, test, lint, run, etc.).
payload.title: Set up mise.toml for toolchain and transient dependency management

payload.description: Create the initial module layout in src/. For POC we need a flat but clean structure: src/main.rs (CLI entry point with index and query subcommands), src/embedder.rs (Candle BGE model loading and inference), src/store.rs (LanceDB table creation, insertion, and query), src/chunker.rs (minimal text chunking logic), src/error.rs (error types from brain-0de.12). Define clean public function signatures between modules: embedder exposes load() and embed_batch(), store exposes create_or_open(), insert_chunks(), and query(), chunker exposes chunk_file(). Keep it simple — no traits or abstractions yet, just functions. ASYNC RUNTIME: Configure tokio with #[tokio::main] multi-threaded runtime. LanceDB's Rust API is async, so all store functions must be async. Use tokio::fs for file I/O. All public APIs return async futures. Acceptance: modules compile, each has a stub implementation that the main.rs can call, tokio runtime is initialized.
payload.title: Set up project module structure for POC

payload.description: Implement a synchronous directory scanner that walks one or more note directories, reads all *.md files, and returns a Vec of (path, content) tuples. Use walkdir or std::fs::read_dir recursively. Filter to only .md extension files. Skip hidden directories (starting with .). Handle read errors gracefully — log a warning and skip unreadable files, don't abort the whole scan. Accept a Vec<PathBuf> of directories to scan (supporting the multi-directory brain model where a brain can index multiple note paths). For the POC, this will be a single directory passed as a CLI arg. Acceptance: given directories with mixed file types, returns only .md file paths and their full text content.
payload.title: Implement one-shot vault scanner

payload.description: Implement a minimal chunking function that splits Markdown content into chunks suitable for embedding. Strategy: split on double newlines (paragraph boundaries) as the primary delimiter. Optionally split further on heading boundaries (lines starting with #). Each chunk should be trimmed of excess whitespace. Filter out empty chunks. Cap chunks to a reasonable max length (~2000 chars, since BGE-small handles 512 tokens ≈ ~1500-2000 chars). For chunks that exceed the cap, split on the nearest sentence boundary. Each chunk gets a chunk_ord (0-indexed position in the file) for ordering. Return Vec<Chunk> where Chunk = { ord: usize, content: String }. Do NOT implement heading path tracking, byte offsets, or token estimation yet — those come in Phase 2. Acceptance: representative markdown files produce sensible, non-empty chunks that fit within the token limit.
payload.title: Implement minimal double-newline/heading chunker

payload.description:
Implement loading the BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5 model locally using Candle. Model weights must be pre-downloaded to disk (see brain-0de.11 for download/caching strategy). The model directory should contain: config.json, tokenizer.json, and model.safetensors.

Steps: (1) Read and parse config.json into candle_transformers::models::bert::Config. (2) Load tokenizer from tokenizer.json using the tokenizers crate. (3) Memory-map safetensors weights using VarBuilder::from_mmaped_safetensors for fast startup and low copy overhead. (4) Construct BertModel::load(vb, &config). (5) Validate hidden_size == 384 from config.

Wrap everything in a CandleBgeEmbedder struct that holds device, model, tokenizer, and hidden_size. Use Device::Cpu for now (GPU comes much later). Provide clear error messages for missing or corrupt model files (use the error types from brain-0de.12).

CANDLE API NOTE: The candle-transformers crate may change its API across versions. Pin the exact candle version in Cargo.lock and treat any candle upgrade as a potentially breaking change that may alter embedding outputs. Store a model_version identifier (model name + weights file SHA256 hash) in metadata for future drift detection.

Acceptance: model loads from a local directory in <2s on CPU, struct is ready for inference, missing model files produce helpful errors referencing download instructions.

## Notes

CANDLE MATURITY WARNING: The candle-core/candle-nn/candle-transformers crates have an unstable API across releases. Expect experimentation: the BertModel loading API, VarBuilder methods, and tensor operations may differ between versions. Approach this task iteratively — try loading with the pinned version, adapt to whatever API surface exists, and document any deviations from the steps listed in the description. If the API has changed significantly, update this task's description with the actual working approach. This is the single highest technical risk in the project since all downstream embedding work depends on it.
payload.title: Implement Candle BGE-small model loader

payload.description:
Implement the embed_batch method on CandleBgeEmbedder that takes a slice of strings and returns Vec<Vec<f32>> of 384-dimensional normalized embeddings. Steps: (1) Tokenize the batch using self.tokenizer.encode_batch() with padding enabled. (2) Convert token IDs and attention masks to Candle Tensors, unsqueeze to add batch dimension, then cat along dim 0. (3) Create zero-valued token_type_ids (same shape as token_ids). (4) Forward pass: self.model.forward(&token_ids, &token_type_ids, Some(&attention_mask)) -> [B, T, H]. (5) CLS pooling: select [:, 0, :] to get the first token's hidden state -> [B, H]. (6) L2 normalize: v / ||v||₂ per row (sqr -> sum_keepdim(1) -> sqrt -> broadcast_div). (7) Convert to Vec<Vec<f32>> via to_vec2(). This is the BGE-recommended pooling strategy and pairs with LanceDB dot product distance. Handle edge cases: empty input, single item. Acceptance: all output vectors have L2 norm ≈ 1.0 (within 1e-5 tolerance), shape is always (batch_size, 384).

## Notes

CANDLE API DEPENDENCY: This task's implementation depends on the exact Candle tensor API discovered in brain-0de.5. The CLS pooling and L2 normalization steps (tensor slicing, sqr, sum_keepdim, sqrt, broadcast_div) may need adaptation if Candle's tensor API differs from what's described. Wait for brain-0de.5 to document the actual working API before implementing.
NUMERICAL STABILITY: The L2 normalization step MUST clamp the magnitude to a minimum epsilon (1e-12) before dividing. Zero vectors from degenerate all-padding inputs would produce NaN otherwise, silently poisoning the LanceDB index. Implementation: let mag = v_sq.sum_keepdim(1)?.sqrt()?.clamp(1e-12, f32::MAX)?; Add a debug assertion that all output vectors have L2 norm within 1e-5 of 1.0. Reference: Stage 1 research report (Concept 3).
payload.title: Implement CLS pooling + L2 normalization embedding

payload.description: Implement LanceDB storage for the POC. Create or open a 'chunks' table with Arrow schema: chunk_id (Utf8, not null), file_path (Utf8, not null), chunk_ord (Int32, not null), content (Utf8, not null), embedding (FixedSizeList(Float32, 384), not null). LanceDB treats FixedSizeList<Float32> columns as vector columns automatically. Implement: (1) open_or_create_table(): connect to local LanceDB URI (directory), open existing 'chunks' table or create with empty RecordBatch if missing. (2) insert_chunks(file_path, chunks, embeddings): build a RecordBatch with chunk_id = '{file_path}:{chunk_ord}', then add to table. For POC, use simple table.add() — merge_insert comes in Phase 1. (3) make_record_batch() helper: construct RecordBatch from chunks + embeddings, properly building the FixedSizeListArray by flattening embeddings into a Float32Array then wrapping. Acceptance: chunks are inserted and the table can be reopened across runs.
payload.title: Implement LanceDB chunks table creation and insertion

payload.description: Implement the query path: take a natural language query string, embed it using the same CandleBgeEmbedder, then search LanceDB for the top-k most similar chunks. Steps: (1) Embed the query text (single-item batch). (2) Call table.vector_search(embedding) with dot product distance metric (optimal for L2-normalized embeddings per LanceDB docs). (3) Set limit(k) where k is configurable (default 5). (4) Execute the query and collect results. (5) Print each result with: rank, score, file_path, chunk_ord, and a snippet of the content (first 200 chars). The output should be clear and human-readable so you can eyeball whether retrieval 'feels right'. Acceptance: querying 'What is X?' against a vault with a note about X returns that note's chunks in the top results.
payload.title: Implement semantic query with top-k retrieval

payload.description:
Wire everything together in main.rs with a minimal CLI. Two subcommands: (1) 'index <notes_path> [--model-dir <path>] [--db <lancedb_uri>]': runs directory scan -> chunk all files -> load embedder -> embed all chunks -> insert into LanceDB. Print progress (files found, chunks created, time elapsed). (2) 'query <query_text> [--k <num>] [--db <lancedb_uri>] [--model-dir <path>]': load embedder -> embed query -> search LanceDB -> print results. Use clap for argument parsing (resolve in brain-0de.1). The model-dir should default to './models/bge-small-en-v1.5'. The db should default to './brain_lancedb'. Log total indexing time and per-file timing at debug level. NOTE: For the POC, <notes_path> is a simple directory path. The brain registry model (brain init, ~/.brain/, multi-directory) comes in Phase 4 (brain-61e.5). The POC should accept note directories as CLI args so it works standalone. Acceptance: cargo run -- index ./notes indexes the directory; cargo run -- query 'my question' returns ranked results.

## Notes

DEPENDENCY NOTE: The clap vs manual argument parsing decision should be resolved in brain-0de.1 (Cargo.toml setup). If clap is included, use it. If not, use std::env::args with clear --help output.
payload.title: Create CLI entry point with index and query subcommands

payload.description: Implement 'brain init' subcommand that creates .brain/brain.toml, registers the brain centrally in ~/.brain/brains/, and sets up the initial directory structure. Referenced in ARCHITECTURE.md lines 55-59.
payload.title: Implement brain init CLI command

payload.description:
Add a daemon subcommand that backgrounds the watcher process. Single global daemon with state at ~/.brain/ (central registry per ARCHITECTURE.md). brain daemon start spawns 'brain watch' as a detached child via Command::new().spawn(), writes PID to ~/.brain/brain.pid, redirects stdout/stderr to ~/.brain/brain.log (append mode). brain daemon stop reads PID file, sends SIGTERM, polls for exit (5s timeout), cleans up PID file. brain daemon status reports whether daemon is running. Uses libc::kill(pid,0) for PID liveness checks (handles ESRCH vs EPERM). Start accepts notes_path arg (like watch). Global args (--model-dir, --lance-db, --sqlite-db) passed through to the re-exec'd brain watch process. Registry-based multi-directory watching is future work.

## Design

Custom daemonize implementation (inspired by daemonize crate, no dependency). Fork/setsid pattern: fork before tokio runtime, child calls setsid() to detach from terminal, redirects stdout/stderr to ~/.brain/brain.log via dup2(), stdin from /dev/null. Parent writes PID file and exits. Child falls through to create tokio runtime and run watch pipeline directly (no re-exec, no command line reconstruction). Main restructured from #[tokio::main] async fn main() to plain fn main() with manual Runtime::new() — required because forking multi-threaded process is UB. Files: crates/cli/Cargo.toml (add libc 0.2, dirs 6), crates/cli/src/commands/daemon.rs (new — daemonize/stop/status + helpers), crates/cli/src/commands/mod.rs (add pub mod daemon), crates/cli/src/main.rs (add DaemonAction enum, restructure main for pre-tokio fork). See plan: .claude/plans/fancy-munching-starfish.md
payload.title: Implement brain daemon start/stop subcommand

payload.body: Implementation note: avoid double-fork daemon pattern. launchd and systemd both expect to manage the process directly — a double-forked daemon fights the service manager. Instead: (1) brain daemon start should spawn 'brain watch' as a detached child via Command::new().spawn(), write PID to .brain/brain.pid, redirect stdout/stderr to .brain/brain.log. (2) brain daemon install (in brain-315) generates a launchd/systemd unit that runs 'brain watch' directly — no daemon wrapper needed. The daemon start subcommand is a convenience for users who don't want to set up a system service; the OS service is the primary path.

payload.description:
Detect and clean orphaned cross-brain task deps and note links when referenced entities no longer exist.

IMPLEMENTATION:
- Add as brain doctor subcommand (brain doctor --check-refs or similar)
- Query all rows where depends_on_brain IS NOT NULL or link_brain IS NOT NULL
- For each unique brain name, open a read-only handle via the registry (~/.brain/config.toml)
- Check if referenced task_id / chunk_id exists in the target brain
- Report dangling refs; optionally remove them with --fix flag

OUTPUT:
- List of dangling cross-brain references with source task, target brain, target ID
- Summary: N refs checked, M dangling found
- With --fix: remove dangling rows from task_deps / task_note_links and emit corresponding DependencyRemoved / NoteUnlinked events

Depends on: cross-brain reference support task (schema must exist first)
payload.title: Cross-brain stale reference cleanup

payload.description: capsule.rs implements a deterministic document summarizer (title + first sentence + heading outline) but it's not called from anywhere. retrieval.rs:139 make_stub() uses first_n_sentences() instead. Wire capsule::generate_capsule into make_stub for higher-quality memory stubs in search_minimal results.
payload.title: Wire capsule module into stub generation

payload.description: Filter by: status, priority (exact + range), type, assignee, label, text search. Support in both CLI and MCP tools. Add FTS on task title + description (reuse SQLite FTS5 pattern from notes).
payload.title: Add per-field task filtering and FTS

payload.description: The tag_match signal in hybrid ranking is currently dead code. In mcp/tools.rs:323, rank_candidates is called with empty query tags: rank_candidates(&candidate_vec, &weights, &[]). The search_minimal MCP schema also doesn't expose a tags parameter. Fix: add optional tags parameter to search_minimal input schema and pass through to rank_candidates.
payload.title: Wire query tags into search_minimal ranking

payload.description:
Generate and install a platform-native service definition so the brain daemon starts automatically on login. On macOS: a launchd plist (~/Library/LaunchAgents/). On Linux: a systemd user unit (~/.config/systemd/user/). Provide brain daemon install and brain daemon uninstall subcommands to manage the service lifecycle. Depends on the daemon start/stop subcommand.

## Notes

Daemon state is centralized at ~/.brain/ (not per-project .brain/). The launchd plist and systemd unit should run 'brain watch' directly (not 'brain daemon start') since service managers expect to manage the process lifecycle themselves. The daemon start subcommand is a convenience for users not using a system service.
payload.title: Add auto-start on login via launchd/systemd

payload.description: Implement lazy (on-demand) loading for optional ML models (reranker, summarizer) to keep daemon baseline memory low. The embedding model (BGE-small, ~130MB) stays hot always — it's needed for every ingest and query. But the reranker and summarizer should only be loaded when first needed, then kept warm with an idle timeout for unloading. Quantized weights (int8/fp16) should be preferred where available to reduce memory footprint. Target: daemon baseline RSS should be ~300-400MB (embedder + SQLite + LanceDB), not 600MB+ with all models loaded.
payload.title: Implement lazy model loading for reranker and summarizer

payload.description: The CLI 'query' command (commands/query.rs) uses raw vector search only. The hybrid ranking engine in ranking.rs already combines 6 signals (vector, BM25, recency, links, tags, importance). Wiring the CLI query through hybrid ranking would improve results for negation queries and overall retrieval quality.
payload.title: Wire CLI query through hybrid ranking engine

payload.description:
MCP tool for appending task events to the JSONL log and updating SQLite projections.

The event log path is resolved from the brain registry: ~/.brain/brains/<name>/tasks/events.jsonl (or a local fallback path in early phases before the registry exists).

Input schema:
- event_type (string, required): one of task_created, task_updated, status_changed, dependency_added, dependency_removed, note_linked, note_unlinked
- task_id (string, optional for task_created — auto-generated; required for all others)
- payload (object, required): event-type-specific fields
  - task_created: { title, description?, priority?, due_ts?, tags? }
  - task_updated: { title?, description?, priority?, due_ts?, tags? }
  - status_changed: { status: open|in_progress|blocked|done|cancelled, reason? }
  - dependency_added/removed: { depends_on_task_id }
  - note_linked/unlinked: { chunk_id }

Processing:
1. Validate input and generate event_id (ULID) and timestamp.
2. Append event as JSON line to the task event log (fsync for durability).
3. Apply event to SQLite projections (update tasks table, task_deps, task_note_links as appropriate).
4. If status_changed to done: check if any blocked tasks are now unblocked, include in response.
5. Return resulting task state + any newly unblocked task IDs.

Response: { event_id, task_id, task: { current state }, unblocked_task_ids?: [...] }

Acceptance: (1) Events are durably appended. (2) Projections are consistent after each event. (3) Dependency cascades (unblocking) are reported.

## Notes

Parent epic: brain-dhi (Phase 2). This is the primary write interface for the task system.
CYCLE DETECTION: When processing dependency_added events, MUST check for cycles before persisting. Run DFS from depends_on_task_id following existing edges — if it reaches task_id, the dependency would create a cycle. Reject with a clear error. This is O(V+E) where V=tasks and E=dependencies, negligible for expected task counts. ULID MONOTONICITY: Same-millisecond events are ordered by ULID's monotonic counter, ensuring deterministic replay. Verify in tests: generate 100 events in a tight loop and confirm they sort in creation order.
payload.title: Implement tasks.apply_event MCP tool

payload.description: Implement an idle/scheduled consolidation job that runs heavier ML summarization. This complements deterministic capsules (ingest-time) with higher-quality ML summaries generated during idle periods. Strategy: (1) run on a timer (e.g., nightly) or when system is idle (no file events for N minutes); (2) process chunks that only have deterministic capsules but no ML summary yet; (3) prioritize high-importance and frequently-accessed chunks; (4) respect CPU budget — limit batch size, yield to indexer if file events arrive; (5) store ML summaries in the summaries table alongside deterministic capsules. This prevents the #1 laptop killer: eager summarization burning CPU on every ingest.
payload.title: Implement consolidation scheduler for ML summarization

payload.description: Goal: 'Ship it and you won't hate yourself later.' Add schema/data migrations (versioned SQLite + LanceDB), rebuild/repair commands (reindex --full, reindex --file, vacuum, doctor checks), deterministic chunking contract with version tracking, brain registry model (brain init, ~/.brain/ central config, multi-brain support), packaging/config/first-run bootstrap, graceful shutdown, and a regression suite with golden tests and property tests. Success criteria: upgrade versions without breaking data; recover from partial failures; initialize and manage multiple brains.
payload.title: Phase 4 — Release Candidate: Stability, Migration & UX

payload.description:
Add a robust schema migration system for SQLite. Implementation: (1) Create a metadata table: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS schema_meta(key TEXT PK, value TEXT). Store 'schema_version' as an integer. (2) On startup, read current schema_version. (3) Define migrations as an ordered list of (version, sql) pairs — each migration is a set of SQL statements that upgrade from version N-1 to N. (4) Run pending migrations in a transaction: BEGIN -> execute each migration -> UPDATE schema_meta SET value = ? WHERE key = 'schema_version' -> COMMIT. (5) If a migration fails, ROLLBACK and abort with a clear error message showing which migration failed and why. (6) All schema changes from Phase 1 onward should be expressed as migrations, not just CREATE IF NOT EXISTS. This enables safe upgrades when users update the binary. Never drop tables — add columns, create new tables, migrate data. Acceptance: upgrading from any previous schema version to current works cleanly; downgrading is documented as 'not supported, restore from backup'; migration failures roll back cleanly.

## Notes

BLOCKED CONTEXT: This task depends on brain-dhi.8 (MCP server) because the schema migration system must account for all tables introduced through Phase 2, including MCP-related schemas. The MCP server task has a RESEARCH NEEDED flag (rmcp vs hand-rolled JSON-RPC) that must be resolved first, as the chosen approach may introduce additional schema requirements.
payload.title: Implement SQLite schema versioning and migrations

payload.description:
Specify where model weights/tokenizer live on disk, how they are referenced by config, and how to verify integrity (hash or checksum). Define failure behavior when artifacts are missing or mismatched.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Documented cache directory layout and config fields\n- Startup verifies checksums before loading\n- Clear error if artifacts missing/mismatched\n- Tests for missing/incorrect checksum
payload.title: Define model artifact cache layout and checksum verification

payload.description:
Add tests that assert byte_start/byte_end correctness for multi-byte UTF-8, mixed newline styles (LF/CRLF), and headings/code blocks. Ensures provenance spans match original markdown bytes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Tests cover UTF-8 multibyte characters\n- Tests cover CRLF and LF line endings\n- Offsets match exact byte slices from original files
payload.title: Add byte-offset correctness tests (UTF-8 + CRLF)

payload.body: Code review finding: chunker.rs:84-94 split_long_text() slices by byte offset (remaining[..max_len]) which will panic on multi-byte UTF-8 characters (emoji, CJK, accented chars) if the boundary lands mid-character. This should be caught by the byte-offset correctness tests. Consider using floor_char_boundary() or manual char boundary detection.

payload.description:
Add a test harness that loads historical SQLite/LanceDB schema snapshots, runs migrations, and validates invariants (row counts, version markers, critical indexes).

## Acceptance Criteria

- Snapshot fixtures for at least one older schema\n- Automated migration test in CI\n- Invariants asserted post-migration
payload.title: Create migration test harness (schema snapshots)

payload.description:
Ensure ~/.brain directories are created with restrictive permissions and warn/fail when permissions are too open. Document expected permissions for task logs and indexes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Directory creation enforces restrictive permissions\n- Startup checks for overly broad permissions\n- Documentation added
payload.title: Enforce ~/.brain permissions and privacy checks

payload.description: Handle LanceDB schema evolution since LanceDB doesn't have built-in migration support. Implementation: (1) Store a 'lancedb_schema_version' in the SQLite metadata table. (2) On startup, check if the existing LanceDB table schema matches expected. Compare field names, types, and vector dimensions. (3) If mismatch detected: log a warning with details of the difference. (4) For minor additions (new nullable columns): alter table if LanceDB supports it, otherwise trigger rebuild. (5) For breaking changes (vector dimension change, column type change): trigger a full rebuild — drop the table, recreate with new schema, mark all files in SQLite as needing re-index (set content_hash to empty). (6) The rebuild triggers the startup scan to re-index everything on next run. Store the 'chunking_version' here too (see separate task). Acceptance: schema version mismatch is detected; rebuild is triggered automatically; after rebuild, the index is fully reconstructed from source Markdown.
payload.title: Implement LanceDB table schema versioning and rebuild

payload.description: Add administrative commands for manual index management. Commands: (1) 'reindex --full': clear all content_hash values in SQLite (forces re-hash of every file), then run the startup scan pipeline. This is the nuclear option for when something goes wrong. (2) 'reindex --file <path>': clear content_hash for a specific file, then re-index just that file. Useful for debugging. (3) 'vacuum': run SQLite VACUUM (reclaim space), run LanceDB optimize() with aggressive compaction, clean up soft-deleted files older than a threshold (default 30 days). (4) 'doctor': diagnostic checks — report orphan chunks in LanceDB (no matching file_id in SQLite), files in SQLite with no chunks in LanceDB, content_hash mismatches (hash stored vs actual file), FTS5 inconsistencies (run integrity-check). Output a clear report with 'OK' or 'PROBLEM: ...' for each check, and suggest repair commands. Acceptance: reindex --full rebuilds everything correctly; doctor catches all known inconsistency types; vacuum reclaims space.
payload.title: Implement reindex and repair CLI commands

payload.description:
Formalize the chunking algorithm as a versioned contract. Changes to the chunking algorithm cause massive index churn (every chunk changes -> every embedding recomputed -> full re-index), so it must be treated as a breaking change. Implementation: (1) Define CHUNKING_VERSION as a constant (start at 1). (2) Store chunking_version per file in SQLite (new column on files table). (3) On indexing: if the file's stored chunking_version \!= current CHUNKING_VERSION, force re-chunking regardless of content_hash (the content didn't change, but the chunking did). (4) When bumping CHUNKING_VERSION: all files will be re-chunked on next startup scan, but only files that produce different chunk_hashes will be re-embedded (the hash gate at the chunk level prevents unnecessary re-embedding). (5) Document the chunking contract: what constitutes a version bump (splitting strategy changes, heading level changes, max chunk size changes). (6) Log a warning at startup if chunking version changed: 'Chunking version changed from X to Y, N files will be re-chunked'. Acceptance: changing CHUNKING_VERSION triggers per-file re-chunking; unchanged content with unchanged chunking produces zero re-embedding.

## Notes

ALTERNATIVE APPROACH — Content-addressed chunk IDs: The current chunk_id format is {file_id}:{chunk_ord}. An alternative hybrid approach could use {file_id}:{heading_path_hash}:{content_hash}, which is content-addressed: a chunk's ID depends on its position in the heading hierarchy and its content, not its ordinal position. This means inserting a paragraph before another chunk does not change downstream chunk IDs, reducing unnecessary LanceDB churn (delete+insert for all subsequent chunks). Trade-off: more complex ID generation, but less index churn on mid-document edits. Consider evaluating this when locking the chunking contract. Note: do NOT use vault_path in the ID — file_id (UUID v7) already provides rename-stable identity.
payload.title: Lock deterministic chunking contract with versioning

payload.description:
Create a proper configuration system, brain registry, and first-run experience.

BRAIN REGISTRY MODEL:
- Central registry at ~/.brain/config.toml lists all registered brains with their note directory paths.
- Per-brain derived data lives at ~/.brain/brains/<name>/ (brain.db, lancedb/).
- Per-brain config at ~/.brain/brains/<name>/config.toml overrides global defaults.
- Project marker: .brain/brain.toml in a project directory identifies the brain name and relative note paths.
- Source-of-truth files stay in-repo: .brain/brain.toml (marker) and .brain/tasks/events.jsonl (event log) are git-tracked.

CONFIGURATION:
(1) Define a GlobalConfig struct: model_dir, default_debounce_ms, default_max_batch_size, default_token_budgets, log_level, brains (HashMap<String, BrainConfig>).
(2) Define a BrainConfig struct: root (project root path), notes (Vec of note directory paths), model_dir (override), debounce_ms, max_batch_size, token_budgets, hybrid_weights, optimize_interval.
(3) Load from ~/.brain/config.toml (global) merged with ~/.brain/brains/<name>/config.toml (per-brain override).
(4) Allow environment variable overrides: BRAIN_HOME (default ~/.brain), BRAIN_MODEL_DIR, BRAIN_LOG_LEVEL.
(5) Sensible defaults for everything.

BRAIN INIT COMMAND:
(1) brain init [--name <name>] [--notes <path>...]: creates .brain/brain.toml in the current directory.
(2) If no --name provided, derive from directory name.
(3) If no --notes provided, default to current directory.
(4) Register the brain in ~/.brain/config.toml (add entry to [brains] table).
(5) Create ~/.brain/brains/<name>/ directory structure (config.toml).
(6) Scaffold .brain/.gitignore in the project (ignoring brain.db*, lancedb/, models/) so derived data stays untracked while source-of-truth files (brain.toml, tasks/events.jsonl) are git-tracked.
(7) Print: 'Brain "<name>" initialized. Note directories: [...]'

FIRST-RUN BOOTSTRAP:
(1) If ~/.brain/ doesn't exist, create the directory structure.
(2) Check if model files exist in model_dir; if not, print instructions for downloading (URL + expected filenames).
(3) Create SQLite database and LanceDB directory for the brain.
(4) Run initial full scan of all registered note directories.
(5) Print a summary: 'Indexed N files, M chunks. Run brain query "your question" to search.'

BRAIN LIST/REMOVE:
(1) brain list: show all registered brains with their note paths and derived data sizes.
(2) brain remove <name>: unregister a brain from the central registry, optionally delete derived data (--purge flag).

Acceptance: first run with no prior state produces a working setup; brain init creates marker, scaffolds .gitignore, and registers; config file is created; missing model files produce helpful instructions; multiple brains can coexist.
payload.title: Implement configuration, packaging, and first-run bootstrap

payload.body: Query scoping: by default, queries should be scoped to the current project's brain (resolved from .brain/brain.toml marker in cwd). Add a --global or --all flag to search across all registered brains. The central registry at ~/.brain/config.toml already knows all brains, so the query command can resolve which LanceDB/SQLite stores to hit. This enables cross-project search while keeping the default behavior project-local.

payload.description: Ensure clean shutdown without data loss or corruption. Implementation: (1) Handle SIGTERM and SIGINT (via tokio::signal). (2) On signal: set a shutdown flag (AtomicBool or CancellationToken). (3) Stop accepting new watcher events. (4) Drain the indexer work queue: process remaining items with a timeout (default 10s). If timeout exceeded, log warning about N unprocessed items. (5) Run a final SQLite checkpoint (PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)) to flush WAL to main database. (6) Run LanceDB optimize() if there are pending unoptimized rows. (7) Close all connections cleanly. (8) Exit with code 0 on clean shutdown, 1 if items were dropped. (9) Implement a 'force shutdown' on second SIGINT: skip draining, close immediately. Log clearly at each shutdown phase so users can see progress. Acceptance: Ctrl+C produces clean shutdown; no corrupt SQLite or LanceDB state; pending work is either processed or clearly reported as skipped.
payload.title: Implement graceful shutdown

payload.description:
Comprehensive regression suite to prevent regressions across versions.

Golden tests:
(1) Chunker golden tests: known Markdown inputs produce exact expected chunk lists. Store expected outputs as JSON fixtures. Any change to chunking output fails the test (intentional changes require updating fixtures AND bumping CHUNKING_VERSION).
(2) Link extraction golden tests: known inputs produce exact expected link lists.
(3) Hash gating golden test: same content always produces same hash.
(4) Task event replay golden test: given a known events.jsonl fixture, rebuild_task_projections() produces exact expected SQLite state (task statuses, dependency graph, blocked/ready sets). Verify idempotency: replaying twice produces identical results.
(5) Intent weight profile golden test: given known candidates and scores, each intent (lookup/planning/reflection/synthesis) produces the expected ranking order.

Property tests (use proptest or quickcheck crate):
(1) Upsert safety: for any sequence of upsert(file_A), upsert(file_B), upsert(file_A_modified) — file_B's chunks are never affected.
(2) Hash determinism: for any string, hash(s) == hash(s) always.
(3) Chunk completeness: for any Markdown file, concatenating all chunks covers all non-whitespace content.
(4) Roundtrip: index a file, query for its exact content, the file appears in top-3 results.
(5) Budget compliance: for any budget_tokens > 0, search_minimal and expand return used_tokens_est <= budget_tokens.
(6) Task event ordering: for any sequence of task events, replaying in ULID order produces a valid task state (no references to non-existent tasks, no dependency cycles).
(7) Ready/blocked consistency: for any task graph state, ready_set ∩ blocked_set = ∅ and ready_set ∪ blocked_set covers all non-terminal tasks.

Use vector_search_mode = 'exact' (brain-k08) for all golden tests that involve vector retrieval to ensure deterministic results.

Add proptest to Cargo.toml dev-dependencies. Acceptance: all golden tests pass; property tests pass for 1000+ generated cases; CI runs the full suite.
payload.title: Create regression test suite with golden tests and property tests

payload.description: Document operational procedures for users. Sections: (1) Upgrading: what happens on version upgrade (schema migrations run automatically, chunking version changes trigger re-index, LanceDB schema changes trigger rebuild). (2) Backup: recommend backing up brain.db and lancedb/ directory before upgrades; or just re-index from Markdown since it's the source of truth. (3) Recovery: if SQLite is corrupted — delete brain.db and run reindex --full. If LanceDB is corrupted — delete lancedb/ directory and run reindex --full. If both are corrupted — both can be rebuilt from Markdown (this is the beauty of unidirectional sync). (4) Troubleshooting: common issues (model files missing, permissions errors, disk full, stale results) with solutions. (5) Performance tuning: debounce interval, batch size, optimize interval, token budgets. (6) Model management: how to download BGE-small weights, expected directory structure. Acceptance: a user can follow the docs to recover from any failure scenario; upgrade path is clear.
payload.title: Write upgrade and recovery documentation

payload.description:
Validate brain config files (global + per-brain) on startup: expand ~, resolve relative paths, normalize separators, detect missing note roots, and **consolidate overlapping paths** with clear logging. Provide a single validation entrypoint used by CLI and daemon.

PATH SUBSUMPTION:
- Implement a normalize_note_paths() utility that canonicalizes all paths (resolve ~, relative paths, symlinks via std::fs::canonicalize) and removes redundant entries via ancestry checks.
- When a parent path is present, any child paths it subsumes are removed from the array.
- When adding a path already covered by an existing parent, the add is a no-op.
- Apply at config write time (brain init, brain add-notes) so the persisted config stays clean.
- Apply at index time (scanner startup) as a defensive check so redundant paths never cause double-indexing regardless of how the config was written.
- Symlinks are resolved before ancestry comparison. Symlink loops are not followed (walkdir already handles this via max_depth and loop detection).

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- Startup fails fast with actionable error messages for invalid config (missing dirs, unresolvable paths)
- Paths are canonicalized consistently in runtime config
- Overlapping paths are consolidated (not rejected): parent subsumes children
- Adding a child already covered by a parent is a silent no-op
- normalize_note_paths() is shared between config write and index-time code paths
- Unit tests cover: tilde expansion, relative paths, missing paths, parent-subsumes-children, child-covered-by-parent, symlink resolution, duplicate exact paths

## Acceptance Criteria

- Startup fails fast with actionable error messages for invalid config\n- Paths are normalized consistently in runtime config\n- Unit tests cover tilde expansion, relative paths, missing paths, and overlaps
payload.title: Add config validation and path normalization

payload.description: brain task create --title '...' [--description] [--priority] [--type] [--assignee]. brain task list [--status] [--priority] [--type] [--assignee] [--ready] [--blocked]. brain task show <id>. brain task update <id> [--title] [--status] [--priority] [--assignee] [--type]. brain task dep add/remove. brain task link/unlink. JSON output with --json flag.
payload.title: Implement brain task CLI commands

payload.description: Goal: 'Low-latency, resilient, and doesn't corrupt data.' Add batching with backpressure, LanceDB optimize() scheduling, proper concurrency model (separate indexer/query workers, SQLite WAL + connection pool), and observability (indexing/query latency p50/p95, stale hash counts, token metrics). Success criteria: stays fast after weeks of use; no deadlocks; no runaway indexing storms.
payload.title: Phase 3 — Performance & Operational Hardening (pre-RC)

payload.description:
Replace single-file-at-a-time embedding with batched embedding within the debounce window. Implementation: (1) Accumulate changed files from the watcher channel over a configurable batch window (default: match debounce interval, 250-500ms). (2) Collect all chunks from all changed files in the batch. (3) Embed the entire batch in one forward pass (or multiple passes if batch exceeds max_batch_size). (4) Cap max_batch_size (default 32 chunks) to prevent OOM on CPU — BGE-small with 384-dim at batch=32 is ~50MB peak, which is safe for most machines. (5) Implement backpressure: if the embedding queue is full (configurable capacity, default 256 chunks), pause accepting new watcher events until the queue drains. This prevents unbounded memory growth during large vault imports. (6) Coalesce by file_id: if the same file appears multiple times in the batch window, only keep the latest event. Acceptance: batch embedding is measurably faster than one-at-a-time; RAM stays bounded during large vault imports; no events are silently dropped.

## Notes

BLOCKED CONTEXT: This task depends on brain-dhi.8 (MCP server) because the batching/backpressure system must integrate with the daemon's event loop. The MCP server task has a RESEARCH NEEDED flag (rmcp vs hand-rolled JSON-RPC) that must be resolved first, as the MCP framework choice affects how the embedding pipeline integrates with the server's async runtime.
payload.title: Implement embedding batching with backpressure

payload.description: Implement LanceDB optimize() scheduling with dual-trigger strategy: compact after every ~100-500 upserts OR every 5-10 minutes of elapsed time, whichever comes first. Without compaction, queries fall back to brute-force scan on unindexed fragments. Track unoptimized row count and elapsed time since last optimize(). Run compaction, pruning/cleanup, and index update as a single optimize() call. Consider running on a background tokio task to avoid blocking the indexer or query path.
payload.title: Implement LanceDB optimize() scheduling

payload.description: Separate the indexing (write) and querying (read) paths to eliminate contention. Architecture: (1) Indexer worker: owns the write path — receives file events, runs the hash -> chunk -> embed -> upsert pipeline. Single-threaded to avoid SQLite write contention (SQLite allows only one writer at a time even in WAL mode). (2) Query worker(s): handle retrieval requests from MCP — run SQLite reads + LanceDB vector searches. Can be multi-threaded since WAL mode allows concurrent readers. (3) SQLite connection pool: one connection for the indexer (writes), a pool of read-only connections for query workers. Configure with PRAGMA query_only=1 on read connections for safety. (4) LanceDB: Table handles are safe to share across threads (Arc<Table>). (5) Communication: use tokio channels — watcher sends file events to indexer, MCP server sends query requests to query workers. Ensure the indexer and MCP server can run concurrently without deadlocks. Acceptance: queries are not blocked by ongoing indexing; indexing is not blocked by concurrent queries; no deadlocks under load.
payload.title: Implement concurrency model with separate indexer and query workers

payload.body: Code review finding: scan_brain() in scanner.rs performs synchronous blocking I/O (WalkDir + std::fs::read_to_string) and is called from the async full_scan() in pipeline.rs:219. For large note directories this blocks the tokio runtime thread, starving watcher event processing. When implementing the concurrency model, ensure scan_brain is wrapped in spawn_blocking, or restructured to run on its own thread.

payload.description: Implement bounded work queue with file_id coalescing and backpressure. Key behaviors: (1) last-write-wins per file — if a file changes again while queued, replace the pending entry rather than queuing a duplicate; (2) bounded queue depth with configurable max — when full, drop oldest entries (they'll be caught on next scan); (3) batch SQLite writes via single writer lane to avoid contention; (4) batch LanceDB upserts to amortize merge_insert overhead; (5) debounce/coalesce is the first line of defense against watcher storms from git pulls, branch switches, or mass edits.
payload.title: Implement work queue with file_id coalescing

payload.description:
Add structured observability to track system health and performance. NOTE: Basic tracing setup (tracing-subscriber, RUST_LOG, stderr output) was established in Phase 0 (brain-0de.12). This ticket adds ADVANCED observability on top of that foundation.

Metrics to track: (1) Indexing latency p50/p95: time from file event received to LanceDB upsert complete. Use tracing spans with timing. (2) Query latency p50/p95: time from MCP tools/call received to response sent. (3) Stale hashes prevented: count of file events where hash gate skipped re-indexing. (4) Token counts: tokens returned by search_minimal and expand per call. (5) Queue depth: current indexer queue depth. (6) LanceDB unoptimized rows: rows since last optimize(). (7) Dual-store consistency: count of files in 'indexing_started' state (indicates failed writes).

Implementation: (1) Use tracing spans with #[instrument] on key functions. (2) Store rolling metrics in-memory (last 1000 measurements for percentile calculation). (3) Expose metrics via a 'status' MCP tool that returns a JSON health report. (4) Structured JSON log format for machine parsing. (5) Optionally emit metrics on SIGUSR1 for debugging without MCP.

Acceptance: can measure indexing and query latency; can track hash gate effectiveness; metrics visible in MCP status tool and logs.
payload.title: Implement observability: metrics and trace logging

payload.description: Test suite for Phase 3 performance and correctness under load. Tests: (1) Batch embedding: verify batched embedding produces identical results to one-at-a-time. (2) Backpressure: flood the indexer with many files, verify RAM stays bounded and no events silently dropped. (3) Coalescing: send 10 events for the same file rapidly, verify only 1 indexing pass occurs. (4) Concurrent read/write: run indexing and queries simultaneously, verify no deadlocks, no corrupt results, queries return consistent data. (5) Optimize scheduling: insert N rows, verify optimize() fires on schedule, query latency doesn't degrade. (6) Startup scan + watcher race: start watcher while startup scan is running, verify no duplicate processing. (7) Latency benchmark: measure p50/p95 for indexing and querying with criterion. Create a test vault with 100+ files for realistic load testing. Acceptance: all concurrency tests pass; no deadlocks; latency benchmarks establish baseline; RAM stays within expected bounds.
payload.title: Write performance and concurrency tests

payload.description:
Configure LanceDB's IVF-PQ (Inverted File Index with Product Quantization) index for optimal recall/latency tradeoff at scale (100k+ chunks).

IVF-PQ MATH: The index partitions the 384-dim vector space into Voronoi cells (IVF) and compresses vectors using Product Quantization (PQ). Key parameters to tune:
- num_partitions: sqrt(N) is a good starting point (e.g., ~316 for 100k chunks, ~450 for 200k)
- num_sub_vectors: 384/sub_dim — determines compression ratio. 48 sub-vectors of 8 dims each gives 100x compression.
- nprobes: number of partitions to search at query time. Higher = better recall, more latency. Start with 10-20 (covers ~5% of partitions).

EXPECTED PERFORMANCE (from research): ~4% of brute-force latency with 95%+ recall at appropriate nprobes setting. This means <5ms vector search even at 200k chunks.

TUNING APPROACH: Create a benchmark that measures recall@10 vs latency across nprobes values {5, 10, 20, 40} for representative queries. The hybrid scoring pipeline compensates for ANN recall loss — FTS catches chunks that vector search misses due to quantization.

Acceptance: (1) IVF-PQ index is created with documented parameters. (2) Benchmark shows >95% recall@10 at <10ms latency. (3) Parameters are configurable via brain.toml.

Reference: Stage 5 research report (Finding 5 — IVF-PQ ANN Search).
payload.title: Configure IVF-PQ index parameters for LanceDB at scale

payload.description: Collection of bugs, safety issues, and hardening tasks discovered during Phase 1 code review. These are pre-release issues that should be addressed before Phase 2 work begins.
payload.title: Phase 1 bugs and hardening

payload.description: CRITICAL: file_id is interpolated directly into LanceDB filter expression strings via format\!("file_id = '{}'", file_id) at store.rs:131 and store.rs:144. While file_id is currently an internally-generated UUIDv7, this pattern is unsafe by default. If any code path ever allows external input to influence file_id, a crafted value like "' OR 1=1 --" could cause mass deletion of all chunks. LanceDB filter strings are evaluated like SQL WHERE clauses. Fix: validate that file_id matches UUID format before interpolation, or use a safe wrapper function that asserts the value contains only hex digits and hyphens.
payload.title: Fix LanceDB filter string injection in store.rs

payload.description: HIGH: When a file is renamed via pipeline.rename_file() (pipeline.rs:168-193), only the SQLite files.path is updated via handle_rename(). The file_path column stored in every chunk row in LanceDB still contains the old path. This means subsequent Store::query() results return stale/broken file paths in QueryResult.file_path, showing the user paths that no longer exist. Fix options: (a) update the LanceDB file_path column for all chunks of that file_id during rename, (b) resolve file_path from SQLite at query time using file_id as the join key, or (c) accept the inconsistency and document it. Option (a) is simplest and most correct.
payload.title: Fix rename not updating LanceDB file_path column

payload.description: HIGH: store.rs:116 uses assert_eq!(chunks.len(), embeddings.len()) which panics unconditionally in both debug and release builds. If a bug in the embedding layer produces a mismatched count, this crashes the entire process. For the long-running watch daemon, a single corrupted file event would terminate the service. Fix: replace with a proper error return: if chunks.len() != embeddings.len() { return Err(BrainCoreError::VectorDb(format!("chunk/embedding count mismatch: {} vs {}", chunks.len(), embeddings.len()))); }
payload.title: Replace assert_eq! with error return in store.rs upsert_chunks

payload.description: HIGH: In db/files.rs:17-25, when get_or_create_file_id() resurrects a soft-deleted file, it clears deleted_at and resets indexing_state to 'idle', but leaves the old content_hash intact. However, when the file was originally deleted, pipeline.delete_file() hard-deleted all LanceDB chunks for that file_id. If the file is recreated with identical content, the hash gate in pipeline.index_file() sees the stored hash matches and skips re-indexing. Result: the file appears indexed in SQLite but has zero searchable chunks in LanceDB — silent data loss. Fix: clear content_hash during resurrection so the file is always re-indexed: UPDATE files SET deleted_at = NULL, indexing_state = 'idle', content_hash = NULL WHERE file_id = ?1
payload.title: Fix resurrection data loss: clear content_hash on file un-delete

payload.description: MEDIUM: scanner.rs reads full file content into ScannedFile.content during scan_brain(), but pipeline.rs:82 reads the same file again via tokio::fs::read_to_string(path) in index_file(). This is (a) wasteful — every file is read twice, and (b) a TOCTOU race — the file could change between the scan read and the index read, causing the content hash to not match what was actually chunked/embedded. The ScannedFile.content field is never used by full_scan. Fix: either pass ScannedFile.content into index_file to avoid the double read, or stop reading content in the scanner and just collect paths. Since the hash gate in index_file skips unchanged files, collecting paths only is the cleaner approach.
payload.title: Fix double file read and TOCTOU race in scan/index pipeline

payload.description: MEDIUM: watcher.rs:94-103 detects renames by checking if a Modify event has exactly 2 paths (paths.len() == 2). However, the Modify event kind includes multiple sub-kinds (ModifyKind::Name, ModifyKind::Data, ModifyKind::Metadata, etc.). Not all Modify events with 2 paths are renames — some systems emit ModifyKind::Data with duplicate paths. The code should match on ModifyKind::Name specifically to distinguish renames from other modify events. Fix: split the Modify match arm into EventKind::Modify(ModifyKind::Name(_)) for renames and EventKind::Modify(_) for content changes.
payload.title: Fix fragile rename detection in watcher event mapping

payload.description: MEDIUM: When a file is deleted via handle_delete() in db/files.rs:80-98, the files row is soft-deleted (sets deleted_at) and LanceDB chunks are hard-deleted. But the SQLite chunks rows for that file_id remain. The chunks table has ON DELETE CASCADE on file_id, but since files are soft-deleted (not hard-deleted), the cascade never fires. These orphan chunk rows accumulate indefinitely, causing unbounded table growth. Fix options: (a) explicitly DELETE FROM chunks WHERE file_id = ? when soft-deleting a file, (b) add periodic cleanup that removes chunk rows for soft-deleted files, or (c) switch to hard-deleting the files row since the resurrection logic in get_or_create_file_id already handles re-creation by path lookup.
payload.title: Clean up orphan SQLite chunks on file soft-delete

payload.description:
Add optional ONNX Runtime cross-encoder reranker via ort crate. Performance policy: (1) rerank is opt-in per query OR triggered only when fusion confidence is low; (2) rerank only top 10-30 fused candidates, never the full candidate pool; (3) rerank capsules/snippets, not full chunks, to minimize compute; (4) realistic latency budget: 200-500ms on CPU for top-20 candidates; (5) load reranker model lazily (not at daemon startup) to keep baseline memory footprint low; (6) allow quantized weights (int8/fp16) to reduce memory and improve throughput.

## Notes

Parent: post-v1 / deferred. Do not implement until base retrieval quality is validated. The ort crate (Rust bindings for ONNX Runtime) is the recommended approach for cross-encoder reranking.
payload.title: Add optional ONNX Runtime cross-encoder reranker via ort crate

payload.description:
Add a fusion confidence metric that measures agreement between vector and FTS candidate rankings. When confidence is low (vector and keyword rankers disagree significantly), automatically trigger cross-encoder reranking on the top-N fused candidates.

CONFIDENCE FORMULA: confidence = |intersection(top_k_vector, top_k_fts)| / k, where k is typically 3-5. High confidence (>0.6): vector and FTS agree, skip reranking. Low confidence (<0.3): significant disagreement, trigger reranker.

RERANKER POLICY: Cross-encoder reranking runs on capsules/snippets (not full chunks) for the top 10-30 fused candidates. Target latency: 200-500ms on CPU. The reranker model is loaded lazily (not at startup) and unloaded after idle timeout.

This is a post-v1 enhancement. The hybrid engine (brain-dhi.6) should be designed to accept an optional reranking step.

Reference: Stage 2 (IR scoring) and Stage 5 (fusion confidence) research reports.
payload.title: Implement fusion confidence metric for adaptive reranking

payload.description: The weight profile numbers in ranking.rs diverge from ARCHITECTURE.md. Direction is correct but exact values differ (e.g. Lookup: arch says bm25=0.30, impl has 0.40; Synthesis: arch says links=0.35, impl has 0.15). Either update ARCHITECTURE.md to reflect the tuned values, or align the code. Prefer updating the doc since the implementation was likely tuned intentionally.
payload.title: Reconcile weight profile values with ARCHITECTURE.md

payload.description: Currently heading_path (e.g. '# Understanding Vector Embeddings > ## How Embedding Models Work') is stored as metadata but not included in the text sent to the embedder. Prepending it would give the CLS token richer context about what a chunk is about, improving retrieval quality. This would bump CHUNKER_VERSION to 3 and trigger re-indexing.
payload.title: Prepend heading context to embedding input text

payload.description: Implement 'brain doctor' subcommand that checks index health, stale references, sync status, schema versions, and reports issues. Referenced in brain-tkr and ARCHITECTURE.md. Should cover: SQLite integrity, LanceDB index health, orphaned embeddings, stale task references, and configuration validity.
payload.title: Implement brain doctor CLI command

payload.description: Goal: 'It's useful as a second brain, not just semantic grep.' Add structured Markdown parsing (headings, wiki-links, frontmatter), FTS5 keyword search, hybrid ranking (vector + FTS + recency + backlinks + tags) with intent-driven weight profiles, token-budgeted retrieval with minimal-first stubs, MCP server exposing memory tools over stdio JSON-RPC, event-sourced task system with JSONL log and SQLite projections, and task MCP tools (tasks.apply_event, tasks.next). Notes and tasks are parallel subsystems with separate sources of truth and cross-referencing. Success criteria: a decent agent loop that doesn't flood context, you can answer 'what did I decide about X?' reliably, and agents can create/query/complete tasks with dependency tracking.
payload.title: Phase 2 — v1 Feature-Complete: Hybrid Retrieval & Agent Surface

payload.description: Replace the minimal POC chunker with a proper Markdown parser. Use pulldown-cmark (or comrak) for robust Markdown parsing. Implement: (1) YAML frontmatter extraction between --- delimiters — parse into a HashMap<String, Value> for title, tags, date, aliases, type, and arbitrary custom fields. (2) Heading hierarchy tracking: maintain a stack of headings to compute heading_path (e.g., '# Title > ## Section > ### Subsection') for each block of content. (3) Document structure extraction: identify paragraphs, code blocks (with language), lists, blockquotes as typed blocks. (4) Preserve code blocks as atomic units — never split mid-code-block. Handle edge cases: empty files, frontmatter-only files, files with no headings, files with only code blocks. Add pulldown-cmark to Cargo.toml. Acceptance: representative vault files parse into well-structured ASTs with correct heading paths and frontmatter.
payload.title: Implement structured Markdown parser with frontmatter

payload.description:
Wire the expand function as an MCP tool for retrieving full chunk content. Input schema: memory_ids (string[], required — chunk IDs from search_minimal results), budget_tokens (integer, default 2000). Processing: (1) Look up chunks by ID in LanceDB (or SQLite if content is stored there). (2) For each chunk, include: memory_id, content (full text), provenance (file_id, file_path, byte_start, byte_end), token_estimate. (3) Pack chunks greedily by the order provided until budget exhausted. (4) If the last chunk would exceed budget, truncate it with a '[truncated]' marker. Response: { budget_tokens, used_tokens_est, results: [{ memory_id, content, provenance, token_estimate }] }. The byte_start/byte_end enable the agent to cite or open the exact location in the original Markdown file. Acceptance: expand returns full chunk text with accurate provenance; budget is respected; byte offsets correctly map back to the source file.

## Notes

BLOCKED CONTEXT: This task depends on brain-dhi.8 (MCP server) and brain-dhi.9 (search_minimal). The MCP server has a RESEARCH NEEDED flag for rmcp vs hand-rolled JSON-RPC — the MCP framework choice determines how tool handlers are registered and invoked.
payload.title: Implement memory.expand MCP tool

payload.description:
Test suite for Phase 2 feature-complete functionality. Tests: (1) Markdown parsing: frontmatter extracted correctly, heading paths built correctly, code blocks preserved as atomic chunks. (2) Link extraction: wiki-links and markdown links stored in SQLite, backlinks queryable. (3) Event-sourced task system: task creation via tasks.apply_event appends to JSONL log and updates SQLite projections; ready/blocked queries return correct results; dependency cascades work (completing a blocking task unblocks dependents); rebuild_task_projections() from JSONL produces identical SQLite state. (4) FTS5: keyword search returns relevant results, BM25 scores are reasonable, FTS stays consistent after updates. (5) Hybrid ranking: verify that hybrid scores combine signals correctly (mock known scores, check final ranking); intent-driven weight profiles produce different orderings for same candidates. (6) Token budgeting: search_minimal never exceeds budget, expand truncates correctly. (7) MCP round-trip: initialize -> tools/list -> tools/call(search_minimal) -> tools/call(expand) works end-to-end; tasks.apply_event -> tasks.next round-trip works. (8) Retrieval quality: create a vault with known content, verify that 'what did I decide about X?' returns the right note; task capsules appear in search results when semantically relevant. (9) Cross-referencing: task_note_links correctly associate tasks with note chunks; search results include both notes and task capsules in unified ranking. Use fixture files and tempdir isolation. Acceptance: all tests pass; v1 success criteria are mechanically verified.

## Notes

BLOCKED CONTEXT: This task depends on brain-dhi.10 (expand) and brain-dhi.12 (write_episode). brain-dhi.12 has RESEARCH NEEDED flags for episode embedding strategy (same table vs separate in LanceDB, what to embed). These decisions affect what the integration tests need to verify. brain-dhi.13 (reflect) also has RESEARCH NEEDED flags for trigger policy and chain semantics.
DEPENDENCY NOTE: Use the shared test fixtures from brain-0de.14 (tests/fixtures/) for test data. The fixture set includes files with frontmatter, wiki-links, tasks, and code blocks that are specifically designed for Phase 2 parsing and retrieval testing.
payload.title: Write v1 integration and retrieval quality tests

payload.description:
Wire the write_episode function as an MCP tool for recording structured episodic events. This is the write side of the agentic memory loop described in RESEARCH.md. Input schema: episode_id (string, optional — UUID v7; if provided and exists, update; if omitted, generate new), goal (string, required — what the agent was trying to do), actions (string[], required — sequence of actions taken), tool_results (string[], optional — tool call results), outcome (string, required — what happened), tags (string[], optional), importance (float, optional — 0.0-1.0, default 0.5). Processing: (1) Parse and validate input. (2) Generate episode_id if not provided. (3) Compute quick metadata: auto-extract tags from content if not provided, set created_at/updated_at timestamps. (4) Store episode in SQLite summaries table with kind='episode'. (5) Optionally embed the concatenated goal+outcome text and store in LanceDB for semantic retrieval. (6) Return: { episode_id, created_at, tags, importance }. Idempotency: if episode_id is provided and already exists, update the existing episode rather than creating a duplicate. RESEARCH NEEDED: Determine the optimal embedding strategy for episodes — embed goal+outcome only, or embed the full episode text? How does this interact with the existing chunk-based retrieval? Should episodes be stored in the same LanceDB table as document chunks or a separate table? Acceptance: (1) Calling via MCP creates a retrievable episode. (2) Episodes appear in search_minimal results when semantically relevant. (3) Duplicate episode_ids update rather than insert.

## Notes

RESEARCH BLOCKER: Episode embedding strategy must be decided during implementation: (1) Should episodes share the LanceDB chunks table or get their own table? (2) Embed goal+outcome only, or full episode text? (3) How do episodes interact with chunk-based retrieval in search_minimal? This decision affects LanceDB schema and the hybrid ranking engine. Also: this task references storing episodes in a 'summaries' SQLite table that does not yet exist — a new task to create this table schema must be completed first.
payload.title: Implement memory.write_episode MCP tool

payload.description:
Wire the reflect function as an MCP tool for consolidating episodic memories into higher-tier semantic summaries. This is the consolidation step in the agentic memory loop from RESEARCH.md. Input schema: memory_ids (string[], required — episode or chunk IDs to consolidate), reflection_prompt (string, optional — guidance for what to extract/synthesize), budget_tokens (integer, default 500 — max tokens for the resulting summary). Processing: (1) Retrieve the specified memories (episodes and/or chunks) from storage. (2) Return the raw content to the calling LLM as a structured prompt asking it to synthesize a summary. (3) The LLM generates the summary and calls reflect again with the summary text (or a separate store_reflection endpoint). (4) Store the reflection in SQLite summaries table with kind='reflection', linking to source memory_ids via a reflections_sources junction table. (5) Optionally embed the reflection text and store in LanceDB. IMPORTANT DESIGN DECISION: The reflect tool does NOT run a local generative model — Candle BGE is an embedding model, not a generative one. Instead, reflect follows a two-step pattern: (a) the tool returns source material to the orchestrator LLM, (b) the LLM synthesizes and calls back to store the result. This keeps the system local-only for storage while leveraging the existing LLM for generation. RESEARCH NEEDED: (1) What is the optimal trigger policy? Options: agent calls explicitly after task completion, periodic background consolidation (requires a timer), or threshold-based (when cumulative importance of un-reflected episodes exceeds N). (2) How should reflection chains work? Can a reflection reference other reflections? (3) Should reflections replace or supplement the source episodes in search results? (4) What SQLite schema additions are needed (reflections table, sources junction table)? Acceptance: (1) Calling reflect with episode IDs returns source content formatted for LLM synthesis. (2) Storing a reflection creates a durable, searchable memory object. (3) Reflections appear in search_minimal with kind='reflection'. (4) Source provenance (which episodes were consolidated) is preserved.

## Notes

RESEARCH BLOCKER: Reflection trigger policy, chain semantics, and storage strategy must be decided: (1) Trigger policy: explicit agent call only, periodic background, or threshold-based? (2) Can reflections reference other reflections (chains)? (3) Should reflections replace or supplement source episodes in search results? (4) What SQLite schema additions are needed (reflections table, sources junction table)? This task also depends on the summaries table schema being created first, and on the episode embedding strategy decision from brain-dhi.12.
payload.title: Implement memory.reflect MCP tool

payload.description: Extend Cargo.toml with Phase 2 dependencies: pulldown-cmark (or comrak) for robust Markdown parsing, ulid (for task event IDs — monotonic, sortable, used in the JSONL event log), serde with derive feature if not already present. Also evaluate and add the rmcp crate (Rust MCP SDK) if the research in brain-dhi.8 determines it is mature enough — otherwise add jsonrpc-core or plan for minimal hand-rolled JSON-RPC. Keep all existing Phase 0 and Phase 1 deps. Verify no version conflicts. Acceptance: cargo check passes with all new deps resolved.
payload.title: Add Phase 2 dependencies to Cargo.toml
