payload.description: Create the summaries table in SQLite that is referenced in RESEARCH.md but missing from the implementation plan. This table stores agentic memory tiers: episodes (kind='episode'), reflections (kind='reflection'), and semantic summaries (kind='summary'). Schema: summary_id (TEXT PK), file_id (TEXT FK nullable — null for agent-generated memories), kind (TEXT NOT NULL — 'episode', 'reflection', 'summary'), title (TEXT), content (TEXT NOT NULL), tags (TEXT — JSON array), importance (REAL DEFAULT 0.5), created_at (TEXT NOT NULL), updated_at (TEXT NOT NULL), valid_from (TEXT nullable), valid_to (TEXT nullable — for belief updating/temporal modeling per RESEARCH.md). Also create a reflection_sources junction table: reflection_id (TEXT FK), source_id (TEXT — episode or chunk ID), linking reflections to their source memories. Add as a SQLite migration. This table is required by brain-dhi.12 (write_episode) and brain-dhi.13 (reflect). Acceptance: summaries and reflection_sources tables exist in SQLite, can insert/query episodes and reflections with proper foreign key constraints.
payload.title: Create SQLite summaries table schema for episodic/semantic memory

payload.description: Create a shared token estimation module used consistently across all retrieval and budgeting code. Multiple tasks reference token estimation (brain-dhi.2 chunking, brain-dhi.7 token-budgeted retrieval, brain-dhi.9 search_minimal, brain-dhi.10 expand) but none implement a shared utility, risking inconsistent implementations. Implementation: (1) Create src/tokens.rs with estimate_tokens(text: &str) -> usize. (2) Primary strategy: use the BGE tokenizer (already loaded in the embedder) for accurate counts. Provide a TokenEstimator struct that holds a reference to the tokenizer. (3) Fallback strategy: chars/4 heuristic when tokenizer is unavailable (e.g., during parsing before embedder is loaded). (4) Make the estimator injectable so tests can use the heuristic without loading the model. (5) Add a estimate_tokens_batch(texts: &[&str]) -> Vec<usize> for efficiency. Acceptance: (1) Token estimates are within 10% of actual tokenizer output for typical Markdown content. (2) All retrieval code uses this shared utility instead of inline estimation. (3) The heuristic fallback is clearly documented as approximate.
payload.title: Implement shared token estimation utility

payload.description:
Write the deterministic chunking contract (inputs, normalization rules, heading boundaries, max size, byte offsets) and introduce a chunker_version field stored with chunks. This unblocks stable IDs and future migrations.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Chunking rules documented in-repo\n- chunker_version persisted with chunk metadata\n- Any change to chunking increments version and triggers reindex
payload.title: Define deterministic chunking contract and versioning (Phase 2)

payload.description:
Implement external-content FTS5 triggers for insert/update/delete and add a consistency check path (doctor/reindex) to rebuild FTS content from chunks when out of sync.

## Acceptance Criteria

- FTS5 triggers created and verified\n- Rebuild path exists to rehydrate FTS content\n- Tests assert FTS results stay consistent after edits/deletes
payload.title: Implement FTS5 triggers and consistency checks

payload.description: Upgrade chunking to be heading-aware with full provenance tracking. Strategy: (1) Primary split on heading boundaries (configurable: split on h2 by default, grouping content under its heading). (2) Each chunk inherits the heading_path from the parser (for context when retrieved). (3) Enforce max chunk size (~400 tokens for BGE-small's 512 limit, leaving headroom for query prefix). (4) Long sections: split further on paragraph boundaries, preserving heading context in each sub-chunk. (5) Track byte_start and byte_end offsets for each chunk relative to the original file (enables 'quotable spans' and provenance in Phase 2 MCP tools). (6) Compute token_estimate per chunk using the BGE tokenizer (accurate) or chars/4 heuristic (fast fallback). (7) Generate chunk_hash (BLAKE3 of chunk content) for fine-grained change detection. Return Vec<Chunk> where Chunk includes: ord, content, heading_path, byte_start, byte_end, token_estimate, chunk_hash. Acceptance: chunks respect heading boundaries, never exceed token limit, have accurate byte offsets that can be used to extract the exact text from the original file.
payload.title: Implement heading-aware semantic chunking with byte offsets

payload.description:
Extract all links from Markdown files and store them in SQLite for graph-based retrieval scoring. Create a links table in SQLite: link_id (TEXT PK), source_file_id (TEXT FK), target_path (TEXT), link_text (TEXT), link_type (TEXT: 'wiki', 'markdown', 'external'). Extract: (1) Wiki-links: [[target]] and [[target|display text]] — regex or parser-based extraction. (2) Markdown links: [text](url) — distinguish internal (.md paths) from external (http/https). (3) Compute a backlinks view or materialized query: for any file, find all files that link TO it. Store on each indexing pass: delete old links for the file, insert new links. The backlink count is used in hybrid scoring (Phase 2): well-linked notes are likely more important. Acceptance: [[note-name]] links are extracted and queryable; get_backlinks('file_id') returns all files linking to it; link graph updates correctly on re-index.

## Notes

GRAPH MODEL: The links table implements a directed graph as an adjacency list (src_chunk_id → dst_chunk_id). Wiki-links are directional: [[target]] creates an edge from the containing chunk to the target chunk. The backlink count used in hybrid scoring (g(links) = log(1+L)/log(1+max_L)) is the in-degree of each node. This adjacency list structure supports the 1-hop BFS expansion in brain-05l. Reference: Stage 4 research report.
payload.title: Implement wiki-link and Markdown link extraction

payload.description:
Replace the Markdown checkbox parsing approach with an event-sourced task system where the JSONL event log is the source of truth for tasks.

DESIGN DECISION: Notes and tasks are parallel subsystems with separate sources of truth:
- Notes: Markdown files (in registered note directories) → SQLite + LanceDB indexes (existing pipeline)
- Tasks: ~/.brain/brains/<name>/tasks/events.jsonl → SQLite projections (this task)
- Cross-references: tasks can link to notes (by chunk_id/file_id) and notes can mention task IDs, but there is no automatic parsing of Markdown checkboxes into tasks.

The task event log lives in the central brain registry (~/.brain/brains/<name>/tasks/), NOT in project directories. This means:
- Task history is portable and centrally managed
- Task data persists even if note directories are moved or removed
- Each brain has its own independent task graph

EVENT LOG (~/.brain/brains/<name>/tasks/events.jsonl):
- Append-only, one JSON object per line
- Each event has: event_id (ULID, monotonic + sortable), task_id, timestamp, event_type, payload, actor
- Event types: task_created, task_updated, status_changed, dependency_added, dependency_removed, note_linked, note_unlinked
- The log is the durable truth. SQLite projections are derived and rebuildable by replaying the log.

SQLITE PROJECTIONS (derived read model, in ~/.brain/brains/<name>/brain.db):
- tasks(task_id PK, title, description, status, priority, due_ts, blocked_reason, created_at, updated_at, capsule_text, capsule_hash)
- task_deps(task_id, depends_on_task_id, PK(task_id, depends_on_task_id))
- task_note_links(task_id, chunk_id, PK(task_id, chunk_id))
- task_events(event_id PK, task_id, ts, event_type, payload_json, actor) — full event timeline for inspection

Status values: open, in_progress, blocked, done, cancelled.
Priority values: 0 (critical) through 4 (backlog).

REPLAY/REBUILD: Implement a rebuild_task_projections() function that truncates all task tables and replays events.jsonl from the start. This is called on first run or when projections are detected as corrupt.

READY/BLOCKED LOGIC:
- Ready set: status IN (open, in_progress) AND all dependencies are done
- Blocked set: at least one dependency not done, or explicit blocked_reason set
- Tie-breaking (deterministic): priority ASC, due_ts ASC NULLS LAST, updated_at DESC, task_id ASC

POC NOTE: For the POC and MVP phases (before brain registry exists in Phase 4), use a local .brain/tasks/events.jsonl path relative to the note directory. The task system should accept a configurable tasks_dir path so it works with both local paths (early phases) and the central registry path (Phase 4+).

Acceptance: (1) Events are appended atomically to JSONL. (2) SQLite projections match log state after replay. (3) Ready/blocked queries return correct results. (4) task_note_links enable cross-referencing without coupling.

## Notes

GRAPH THEORY CONTEXT: The task dependency graph is a DAG (directed acyclic graph). The ready/blocked logic is equivalent to finding vertices with in-degree 0 in the subgraph of incomplete tasks. CYCLE PREVENTION is critical: adding a dependency that creates a cycle makes tasks permanently blocked with no possible resolution. The dependency_added event handler MUST validate acyclicity before persisting — use DFS from the target through existing edges in O(V+E). ULID ORDERING: Event IDs use ULIDs which provide monotonic ordering within the same millisecond via an incrementing random component. This guarantees deterministic replay order even under concurrent event generation. Reference: Stage 4 (graph theory) and Stage 5 (ULID) research reports.
payload.title: Implement event-sourced task system with JSONL log

payload.description:
Add full-text search over chunk content using SQLite FTS5 in external-content mode. This ticket owns the FTS5 schema (virtual table + triggers) since it is NOT part of the Phase 1 SQLite schema.

Steps: (1) Add a 'content' TEXT column to the SQLite chunks table (store chunk text here as well for FTS — this is the pragmatic choice since FTS5 external-content needs a backing table). This requires a schema migration from the Phase 1 chunks table. (2) Create FTS5 virtual table: CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE fts_chunks USING fts5(content, title, tags, content='chunks', content_rowid='rowid'). (3) Create INSERT/DELETE/UPDATE triggers on chunks that keep fts_chunks in sync automatically. (4) Implement search_fts(query) -> Vec<(chunk_id, bm25_rank)> using FTS5's built-in rank function. (5) Normalize BM25 scores to [0, 1] range for hybrid scoring compatibility (divide by max BM25 score in result set, or use min-max normalization). Support phrase queries ('exact phrase') and boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) via FTS5 query syntax.

SCHEMA OWNERSHIP: This ticket creates the fts_chunks virtual table and its maintenance triggers. The Phase 1 schema (brain-ffh.2) does NOT include FTS — that is intentionally deferred to this ticket.

Acceptance: keyword search returns relevant chunks ranked by BM25; FTS stays consistent after chunk upserts and deletes (verified via triggers); can combine FTS results with vector results in the hybrid ranking engine.
payload.title: Implement SQLite FTS5 external-content full-text search

payload.description:
Combine multiple signals into a single relevance score for retrieval. Hybrid formula: S = w_v * sim_v + w_k * bm25 + w_r * f(dt) + w_l * g(links) + w_t * tag_match + w_i * importance.

CROSS-STORE QUERY ORCHESTRATION: The hybrid engine must merge candidate sets from two different stores:
- Phase 1: Retrieve a generous candidate set from LanceDB (top-50 by vector similarity via dot product).
- Phase 2: Query SQLite FTS5 for keyword matches (top-50 by BM25).
- Phase 3: Union the two candidate sets, deduplicate by chunk_id.
- Phase 4: Enrich with SQLite metadata in a single batch query: SELECT backlink_count, tags, updated_at, importance FROM chunks JOIN ... WHERE chunk_id IN (...).
- Phase 5: Compute hybrid score for each candidate.
- Phase 6: Sort by hybrid score, return top-k.

Implementation of each signal: (1) sim_v: LanceDB dot product similarity score (already in [0,1] for normalized embeddings). (2) bm25: SQLite FTS5 BM25 rank, normalized to [0,1] by dividing by the max BM25 score in the result set. (3) f(dt) = exp(-dt/tau): recency decay where dt = seconds since updated_at. Use tau = 30 days as default (configurable). (4) g(links) = log(1 + backlink_count) / log(1 + max_backlinks): normalized backlink score. (5) tag_match: Jaccard coefficient between query tags and chunk tags. (6) importance: set to 1.0 for all chunks initially (proper importance scoring deferred post-v1).

Start with equal weights (all 1/6) and a simple linear combination. Don't overcomplicate: the weights are configurable and can be tuned later.

Acceptance: hybrid search returns better results than vector-only or keyword-only for mixed queries; the union approach catches chunks that score high on keywords but low on vector similarity (and vice versa).

## Notes

FUTURE ENHANCEMENT — Graph expansion: After this task is complete, brain-05l adds 1-hop graph expansion as a third candidate source (alongside vector and FTS). The hybrid engine should be designed with an extensible candidate pipeline so graph expansion slots in cleanly. The current Phase 6 (sort by hybrid score) should accept candidates from any source.

FUTURE ENHANCEMENT — Intent-driven weight profiles: brain-dhi.9 adds an intent parameter that maps to different weight profiles for the hybrid formula. Design the weight configuration to be a simple struct/map that can be swapped per-query.
MATHEMATICAL INVARIANT — Weight normalization: All intent weight profiles MUST sum to 1.0. Validate at config load time with a debug assertion. The research phase discovered that hand-tuned profiles can silently drift (e.g., a profile summing to 1.1), which biases retrieval toward over-weighted signals without any runtime error. Implement a normalize_weights() utility that divides each weight by their sum, and call it at profile load time. BM25 NORMALIZATION: The bm25 signal uses min-max normalization (divide by max BM25 in result set). Handle the edge case where max_bm25 == 0 (no FTS matches) by setting all bm25 scores to 0.0 rather than dividing by zero. JACCARD COEFFICIENT: The tag_match signal uses Jaccard(A,B) = |A∩B| / |A∪B|. Handle empty sets: Jaccard(∅,∅) should return 0.0, not NaN. Reference: Stage 2 and Stage 5 research reports.
payload.title: Implement hybrid ranking engine

payload.description: Implement the two-tier retrieval API that keeps agent context tight. (1) search_minimal(query, filters, budget_tokens, k): run hybrid retrieval, but return only MemoryStub objects — memory_id, kind, title (first heading or filename), summary_2sent (first ~2 sentences of the top chunk), scores object (hybrid, vector, keyword, recency, links), provenance (file_id, file_path), and expand_hint (tool name + args to get full content). Pack stubs greedily by score until budget_tokens is exhausted. Include used_tokens_est in the response. (2) expand(memory_ids, budget_tokens): given specific chunk/memory IDs, return the full chunk content with byte offsets for provenance. Enforce budget strictly — truncate the last chunk if needed. Include used_tokens_est. Token estimation: use the tokenizer for accuracy, or chars/4 as fast heuristic. Every response must include: used_tokens_est, the number of results returned, and the total results available (so the agent knows if there's more). Acceptance: search_minimal returns compact stubs within budget; expand returns full text within budget; total tokens never exceed the specified budget.
payload.title: Implement token-budgeted minimal-first retrieval

payload.description:
Implement the MCP server that exposes memory tools to AI agents over stdio JSON-RPC. RESEARCH NEEDED: Evaluate whether to use the official Rust MCP SDK (rmcp crate / modelcontextprotocol/rust-sdk on GitHub) vs hand-rolling JSON-RPC. The rmcp SDK handles the lifecycle handshake, timeout management, and protocol compliance automatically. If rmcp is mature enough, use it. If not, use jsonrpc-core or implement minimal JSON-RPC manually.

MCP protocol requirements: (1) Read newline-delimited JSON-RPC messages from stdin. (2) Write JSON-RPC responses to stdout. (3) Handle the MCP lifecycle: 'initialize' (return server info and capabilities, negotiate protocol version), 'initialized' (acknowledgment — only then accept tool calls), 'tools/list' (return tool definitions with JSON Schema), 'tools/call' (dispatch to tool handlers). (4) Request timeouts: implement timeouts for all in-flight requests per MCP spec. (5) Graceful shutdown on EOF (stdin closed): flush pending writes, checkpoint SQLite WAL, close LanceDB handles.

Implement tool definitions for: memory.search_minimal, memory.expand, memory.write_episode, memory.reflect. Each tool definition includes: name, description, inputSchema (JSON Schema with all parameters, types, defaults, constraints). Keep schemas stable early — changing tool schemas after agents depend on them is painful.

LOGGING: Since stdout is consumed by MCP JSON-RPC, all tracing/logging MUST go to stderr. Configure tracing-subscriber accordingly.

DAEMON INTEGRATION: This ticket transitions the binary from a one-shot CLI (Phase 0/1) to a long-lived daemon. The MCP server runs alongside the file watcher and indexer. Use tokio::select! to multiplex MCP stdin reading, file watcher events, and shutdown signals.

Acceptance: an MCP client can connect via stdio, complete the initialize handshake, list tools, and call memory.search_minimal/expand with correct results. Malformed input returns JSON-RPC error, doesn't crash.

## Notes

RESEARCH BLOCKER: The rmcp (Rust MCP SDK) vs hand-rolled JSON-RPC decision is a binary architectural choice that determines the entire MCP implementation approach. This must be researched at implementation time: evaluate rmcp crate maturity (lifecycle handshake, timeout management, protocol compliance). If rmcp is immature, fall back to jsonrpc-core or minimal hand-rolled JSON-RPC. All downstream MCP tool tasks (dhi.9, dhi.10, dhi.12, dhi.13) and Phase 3/4 tasks (7ex.1, 61e.1) are blocked on this being resolved.
payload.title: Implement MCP stdio JSON-RPC server

payload.description:
Wire the search_minimal retrieval function as an MCP tool. Input schema: query (string, required), intent (string, optional, one of: 'lookup' | 'planning' | 'reflection' | 'synthesis' | 'auto', default 'auto'), filters (object, optional: tags_any: string[], kind_any: string[], updated_after: string ISO8601, file_id: string), budget_tokens (integer, default 800), k (integer, default 10).

INTENT-DRIVEN RETRIEVAL: The intent parameter adjusts retrieval behavior per use case:
- lookup (fact finding): Prefer lexical precision (upweight FTS/BM25), return small snippets with direct citations. Lower k, higher precision.
- planning (what to do next): Prefer task graph + procedural playbooks + recent episodic outcomes. Upweight recency and links. Return capsules and dependency edges.
- reflection (what happened, how did we decide): Prefer episodic event stream + decision records. Upweight recency. Return timelines and references.
- synthesis (write or design something): Prefer semantic notes and consolidated capsules. Allow wider recall (higher k), keep raw text gated behind expansion.
- auto (default): Use a simple heuristic or the hybrid engine defaults. No weight adjustment.

Each intent maps to a weight profile for the hybrid scoring formula (w_v, w_k, w_r, w_l, w_t, w_i). For v1, implement as a simple lookup table of weight presets per intent. Configurable override via config file.

Processing: (1) Parse and validate input including intent. (2) Resolve intent to weight profile (or use defaults for 'auto'). (3) Apply filters to narrow the SQLite prefilter (if filters provided). (4) Run hybrid retrieval with intent-adjusted weights. (5) Build MemoryStub array from results. (6) Pack stubs within budget_tokens. Response: { budget_tokens, used_tokens_est, intent_resolved, result_count, total_available, results: [{ memory_id, kind, title, summary_2sent, scores: { hybrid, vector, keyword, recency, links }, provenance: { file_id, file_path }, expand_hint }] }.

This is the primary retrieval tool — optimized for minimal token footprint while giving the agent enough signal to decide what to expand. Acceptance: calling via MCP returns properly structured stubs; budget is respected; filters narrow results correctly; intent parameter adjusts weight profiles.

## Notes

WEIGHT PROFILE VALIDATION: When resolving intent to weight profile, validate that weights sum to 1.0 (or normalize them). Log a warning if a config-provided profile doesn't sum to 1.0 before normalizing. This catches configuration errors early. Reference: See brain-dhi.6 notes on weight normalization invariant.
payload.title: Implement memory.search_minimal MCP tool

payload.description: Epic tracking minor quality improvements identified during Phase 2 implementation review. All gaps are non-blocking — the core hybrid retrieval pipeline is correct. These are quality/completeness improvements.
payload.title: Phase 2 Polish: Minor Gaps from Implementation Review

payload.description: Goal: 'It stays correct while I use it daily.' Add SQLite control plane with hash-gated incremental indexing, LanceDB merge_insert upsert semantics with per-file scoped deletion, and file watching with debouncing via notify-debouncer-full. Support watching multiple note directories per brain. Success criteria: save a file repeatedly, rename it, delete it — index stays consistent and queries don't show ghosts.
payload.title: Phase 1 — MVP: Correctness & Incremental Updates

payload.description: Extend Cargo.toml with Phase 1 dependencies: rusqlite (0.32+, features: bundled) for SQLite with compiled-in SQLite3, blake3 (1.x) for fast content hashing, uuid (1.x, features: v7, serde) for time-ordered file identity, notify (6.x) and notify-debouncer-full (0.7+) for filesystem watching with event coalescing. Also add serde (1.x, features: derive) and serde_json (1.x) if not already present. Keep all existing POC deps. Verify no version conflicts with existing arrow/lancedb stack. Acceptance: cargo check passes with all new deps resolved.
payload.title: Add SQLite and incremental indexing dependencies to Cargo.toml

payload.description:
Comprehensive tests for Phase 1 correctness guarantees. Tests: (1) Hash gate: index a file, re-index without changes — SQLite last_indexed_at should NOT update. (2) Hash gate: modify file content — last_indexed_at updates, new chunks appear. (3) Upsert correctness: add a paragraph to a file — new chunk in LanceDB, others unchanged. (4) Upsert correctness: remove a paragraph — orphan chunk deleted from LanceDB. (5) Cross-file safety: re-index file A does not affect file B's chunks. (6) File deletion: delete a file — soft-deleted in SQLite, chunks gone from LanceDB, queries return no results for that file. (7) File rename: rename a file — same file_id, updated path, queries work with new path. (8) Startup scan: offline changes are detected and processed. (9) Debounce: rapid saves produce single indexing pass. Use tempdir for test isolation. Create helper functions for common setup (init vault, create files, run indexing). Acceptance: all tests pass; the MVP success criteria are mechanically verified.

## Notes

DEPENDENCY NOTE: Use the shared test fixtures from brain-0de.14 (tests/fixtures/) for test data. Extend the fixture set if Phase 1 testing needs specific edge cases (e.g., files designed to trigger hash gate, rename scenarios).
payload.title: Write MVP integration tests

payload.description: Create the SQLite control plane schema. Tables: (1) files(file_id TEXT PK, path TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL, content_hash TEXT NOT NULL, mtime TEXT, size INTEGER, last_indexed_at TEXT NOT NULL, deleted_at TEXT nullable) — file identity and hash gate. (2) chunks(chunk_id TEXT PK, file_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES files, chunk_ord INTEGER NOT NULL, chunk_hash TEXT NOT NULL, token_estimate INTEGER, byte_start INTEGER, byte_end INTEGER) — chunk metadata (content lives in LanceDB, not duplicated here for POC). Create an init_db() function that opens SQLite at a given path, enables WAL mode (PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL), enables foreign keys, and runs CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS for both tables. Use a simple migration pattern: store a schema_version in a metadata table, check on startup, run migrations sequentially. Acceptance: database initializes cleanly on first run, reopens on subsequent runs, WAL mode confirmed.
payload.title: Design and implement core SQLite schema

payload.description: Implement stable file identity that survives renames. get_or_create_file_id(conn, path) -> file_id: query files table by path; if found, return existing file_id; if not, generate a new UUID v7 (time-ordered for nice sorting) and insert a new row. handle_rename(conn, old_path, new_path): find file_id by old_path, update path to new_path. This preserves the file_id so all chunk references remain valid. handle_delete(conn, path): set deleted_at = now() on the file row (soft delete). Do NOT hard-delete — this allows detection of 'ghost' files and cleanup scheduling. Implement path normalization: canonicalize paths to avoid duplicates from symlinks or relative vs absolute paths. Acceptance: same file always returns same file_id; renamed file preserves its ID; deleted file is soft-deleted.
payload.title: Implement file identity management with UUID v7

payload.description:
Implement the hash gate that prevents redundant re-indexing. This is the correctness anchor of the entire system. On each file event: (1) Read file content. (2) Normalize whitespace (optional: trim trailing whitespace per line, normalize line endings to LF) to ensure hash stability across editors. (3) Compute BLAKE3 hash of normalized content. (4) Query SQLite: SELECT content_hash FROM files WHERE file_id = ?. (5) If hash matches: skip this file entirely (log at debug level). (6) If hash differs or file is new: proceed with re-chunking and re-embedding. (7) After successful indexing, UPDATE files SET content_hash = ?, last_indexed_at = datetime('now'). BLAKE3 is chosen for speed (3-4x faster than SHA-256) and simplicity (no length extension attacks matter here). The hash gate prevents 'index storms' from editors that write temp files then rename, or that save multiple times rapidly. Acceptance: editing a file triggers re-index; saving without changes does NOT trigger re-index; verified by checking SQLite bookkeeping timestamps.

## Notes

COLLISION PROBABILITY: BLAKE3 produces 256-bit hashes. For a vault of 10,000 files, the probability of any collision is P < 10^(-70) by the birthday bound — effectively impossible without adversarial construction. The hash gate is the correctness anchor that prevents index storms; its mathematical safety margin is enormous. NORMALIZATION SENSITIVITY: The whitespace normalization step before hashing must be deterministic across all platforms. Document the exact normalization rules (LF line endings, trim trailing whitespace) as part of the versioned chunking contract. Reference: Stage 5 research report (Finding 1).
payload.title: Implement BLAKE3 content hash gate

payload.description:
Replace the simple table.add() from POC with proper merge_insert upsert semantics. This is critical for correctness when files are edited and chunks change.

Use MergeInsertBuilder with chunk_id as the merge key. Configure: (1) when_matched_update_all(None) — update existing chunks that still exist. (2) when_not_matched_insert_all() — insert new chunks that appeared. (3) when_not_matched_by_source_delete(Some(format!("target.file_id = '{}'", file_id))) — delete chunks that disappeared from THIS file only. The file_id filter in the deletion clause is CRITICAL: without it, you'd delete chunks from ALL files, nuking the entire table on every single-file update.

Also update chunk_id format to use file_id instead of file_path (since file_id is stable across renames): chunk_id = '{file_id}:{chunk_ord}'.

RETRY LOGIC: LanceDB's Rust SDK does not auto-retry on CommitConflict errors. Wrap all merge_insert calls in a retry loop: 3 attempts, exponential backoff (100ms, 400ms, 1600ms). Log each retry attempt. If all retries fail, return an error (the indexing state machine in brain-ffh.7 will handle recovery).

SAFETY: Write an integration test that verifies the file_id filter works correctly: upsert(file_A) then upsert(file_B) then upsert(file_A_modified) — file_B's chunks must remain intact. This test catches the catastrophic bug where a filter expression error deletes chunks from other files.

Acceptance: (1) edit a file to add a paragraph: new chunk appears, old chunks preserved. (2) edit a file to remove a paragraph: orphan chunk is deleted. (3) re-index file A: file B's chunks are untouched. (4) CommitConflict errors are retried.

## Notes

ALTERNATIVE APPROACH — Content-addressed chunk IDs: The current chunk_id = '{file_id}:{chunk_ord}' is ordinal-based. A future alternative is {file_id}:{heading_path_hash}:{content_hash}, which avoids re-IDing all downstream chunks when a paragraph is inserted mid-document. This reduces LanceDB merge_insert churn. See brain-61e.4 notes for details. The merge_insert logic here should work regardless of ID format — it keys on chunk_id, so any stable unique string works.
payload.title: Implement LanceDB merge_insert upsert with per-file scoped deletion

payload.description: Add filesystem watching using notify-debouncer-full for event coalescing. This ticket implements ONLY the watcher component that emits filesystem events — it does NOT include hash-gate integration (that is brain-ffh.7's job). Setup: (1) Create a debouncer with configurable interval (default 250ms). (2) Watch all registered note directories recursively (RecursiveMode::Recursive). Support watching multiple directories (one watcher per directory, or a single watcher with multiple watch paths). (3) Filter events to only *.md files. (4) Use a tokio mpsc channel to bridge the sync watcher callback to the async indexing pipeline. (5) In the callback, extract the changed file path from each debounced event and send it to the channel. Event coalescing is important because editors differ: some truncate/write-in-place, others write a temp file then rename. The debouncer stitches rename events and deduplicates rapid create/remove patterns. The watcher emits structured events: FileChanged(path), FileCreated(path), FileDeleted(path), FileRenamed(old_path, new_path). Downstream consumers (brain-ffh.7) decide what to do with these events. Acceptance: saving a .md file in an editor triggers exactly one event on the channel; saving a .txt file is ignored; rapid saves produce one (debounced) event; events carry the correct event type; watching multiple directories works.
payload.title: Implement file watcher with notify-debouncer-full

payload.description:
Connect all Phase 1 components into the complete incremental pipeline: watcher event -> read file -> hash gate (SQLite) -> chunk -> embed -> LanceDB merge_insert upsert -> SQLite bookkeeping update.

CRITICAL: DUAL-STORE CONSISTENCY — Implement an indexing state machine per file in SQLite to handle partial failures across SQLite and LanceDB:
- Before any writes: set file state to 'indexing_started' in SQLite.
- Write SQLite metadata (chunks, links) in a single transaction.
- Write to LanceDB (merge_insert).
- On success: set file state to 'indexed', update content_hash.
- On failure at ANY step: leave state as 'indexing_started'. Do NOT update content_hash.
- On startup: scan for files stuck in 'indexing_started' and re-index them from scratch, ignoring the hash gate.
This prevents the scenario where SQLite records success but LanceDB failed — the file will be retried.

The pipeline must handle: (1) New file: create file_id, hash, chunk, embed, insert into LanceDB, record in SQLite. (2) Modified file: hash gate detects change, re-chunk, re-embed, upsert into LanceDB (merge_insert handles adds/updates/deletes), update SQLite hash. (3) Deleted file: soft-delete in SQLite, delete all chunks for this file_id from LanceDB. (4) Renamed file (if debouncer detects it): update path in SQLite, no re-indexing needed since content hasn't changed.

RETRY LOGIC: Wrap all LanceDB merge_insert calls in a retry loop (3 attempts, exponential backoff) to handle CommitConflict errors. Serialize all LanceDB writes through a single writer task (tokio mpsc channel pattern).

Log each stage with tracing spans. Acceptance: the full loop works end-to-end with real files in an editor; partial failures are recoverable on restart.
payload.title: Wire complete incremental sync pipeline

payload.description: On daemon startup, before the watcher begins, perform a full scan of all registered note directories to catch any changes that happened while the daemon was offline. Steps: (1) Walk all note directories for *.md files (reuse the scanner from Phase 0, which accepts a Vec<PathBuf>). (2) For each file, check content hash against SQLite. (3) Queue changed/new files for indexing through the same pipeline. (4) Detect deleted files: query SQLite for all non-deleted file paths, check which ones no longer exist on disk, soft-delete those. (5) Clean up LanceDB: delete chunks for soft-deleted files. This ensures consistency after offline edits, daemon restarts, or files synced via git/cloud storage while the daemon was down. Log: X files scanned, Y changed, Z deleted, total time. Acceptance: stop daemon, edit 3 files, delete 1 file, restart daemon — all changes reflected correctly.
payload.title: Implement initial full-vault scan on startup

payload.description:
Implement robust handling for file deletions and renames, which are the trickiest filesystem events. This is two distinct sub-problems:

DELETIONS: (1) Detect via watcher (FileDeleted event) or startup scan (file in SQLite but not on disk). (2) Soft-delete in SQLite: UPDATE files SET deleted_at = datetime('now') WHERE path = ?. (3) Remove all chunks from LanceDB: delete where file_id matches. (4) Clean up related SQLite rows: remove from chunks, links/backlinks tables. (5) FTS5 triggers should handle fts_chunks cleanup automatically. (6) Handle partial failure: if LanceDB deletion succeeds but SQLite update fails, the startup scan will catch the inconsistency.

RENAMES: (1) Detect via debouncer's rename stitching (pairs Create+Remove into FileRenamed). (2) Update path in SQLite: UPDATE files SET path = ? WHERE file_id = ?. (3) No re-embedding needed since content hasn't changed and chunk_ids use file_id (not path). (4) Update the links table: any links that reference the old path should be re-resolved.

FALLBACK: If rename detection fails (editor-dependent, especially on Linux inotify), treat as delete + create. The new file gets a new file_id but the content_hash will match, so the hash gate prevents re-embedding if using content-based comparison. However, the old file_id's chunks remain orphaned — the startup scan or a doctor command should detect and clean these up.

EDGE CASES: (a) 'delete then recreate with same name' — should create a new file_id. (b) 'rename A to B, then create new A' — file_id for old A follows to B, new A gets a new file_id. (c) 'save via temp file + rename' pattern (common in editors) — debouncer should handle this as a modify event, not rename.

Acceptance: delete a file — no ghost results in queries; rename a file — queries return results with the new path; editor save patterns don't create duplicate entries.
payload.title: Handle file deletions and renames correctly

payload.description: Auto-configure MCP server for agent frameworks. Detects model path, db path, and writes the correct JSON config to the target's settings file. Targets: claude-code (~/.claude/settings.json), cursor (~/.cursor/mcp.json), vscode (.vscode/settings.json). Should validate paths exist, warn if model not downloaded, and print the generated config.
payload.title: Add 'brain mcp setup <target>' CLI command

payload.description:
Replace the simple logarithmic backlink count (g(links) = log(1+L)/log(1+max_L)) with a PageRank-inspired iterative importance score that accounts for the quality of linking pages, not just quantity.

MOTIVATION: The current backlink score treats all incoming links equally. A chunk linked from a high-importance hub should score higher than one linked from a rarely-referenced leaf. PageRank captures this by iteratively propagating importance through the link graph.

ALGORITHM (simplified PageRank):
1. Initialize all chunks with score 1/N
2. Iterate: score(v) = (1-d)/N + d * Σ(score(u)/out_degree(u)) for all u linking to v
3. d = 0.85 (damping factor), converge when max delta < 1e-6
4. Typically converges in 20-50 iterations for <200k nodes

INTEGRATION: Precompute PageRank scores during LanceDB optimize() cycles (background task). Store as a column in the chunks table. The hybrid scoring formula uses the precomputed score instead of raw backlink count.

This is a post-v2 enhancement. The current log-backlink scoring is sufficient for v1 and v2.

Reference: Stage 4 research report (PageRank analysis).
payload.title: Implement PageRank-inspired link scoring for graph retrieval

payload.description: Add assignee: Option<String> to TaskCreatedPayload and TaskUpdatedPayload. Add assignee column to tasks projection table. Support in list queries (filter by assignee).
payload.title: Add assignee field to task events

payload.description: New event types: LabelAdded { label: String }, LabelRemoved { label: String }. New projection table: task_labels (task_id, label) with composite PK. Support label filtering in queries.
payload.title: Add labels support to task events

payload.description:
Add cycle detection to the task dependency system to prevent circular dependencies that would make tasks permanently blocked.

ALGORITHM: When a dependency_added event arrives (task A depends on task B), run DFS from B through existing dependency edges. If the traversal reaches A, the new edge would create a cycle — reject the event with a descriptive error.

IMPLEMENTATION:
1. Build an adjacency list from task_deps table: HashMap<TaskId, Vec<TaskId>>
2. DFS from depends_on_task_id, tracking visited set
3. If task_id is reachable, return Err(CycleDetected { path: Vec<TaskId> })
4. Include the cycle path in the error for debugging

COMPLEXITY: O(V+E) per dependency_added event, where V=total tasks and E=total dependencies. For expected task counts (<10k), this is sub-millisecond.

FUTURE: This same traversal infrastructure supports topological sort (Kahn's algorithm) for critical path analysis and task scheduling visualization.

Acceptance: (1) Adding A→B→C→A dependency chain fails with CycleDetected error. (2) Valid DAG dependencies succeed. (3) Error includes the detected cycle path. (4) Tests cover diamond dependencies (valid) and self-loops (invalid).
payload.title: Implement cycle detection for task dependency graph

payload.description:
DEFERRED/POST-V1: Parse Markdown '- [ ]' and '- [x]' items during note ingestion and automatically create corresponding task events in the JSONL log.

This bridges the note and task subsystems: when a user writes a checkbox item in a Markdown note, it becomes a real task with full lifecycle support (dependencies, status tracking, agent workflow).

Extraction rules (from original brain-dhi.4):
- Match lines starting with '- [ ] ' (open) or '- [x] ' (done)
- Extract inline due dates: 'due:2026-03-01' or '@due(2026-03-01)'
- Extract inline tags: #tag patterns
- Support nested task lists (indented items → potential dependency hints)
- Track source file_id and line_number for provenance and task_note_link

Challenges to resolve:
- Idempotency: re-indexing a file shouldn't create duplicate tasks. Need stable task identity derived from (file_id, line_number, content_hash).
- Bidirectional state: if an agent marks a task done via tasks.apply_event, should the Markdown checkbox be updated? This would violate notes-as-source-of-truth. Probably not — accept divergence and surface it in the UI.
- Deletion: if a user removes a checkbox line, should the task be cancelled?

This is explicitly deferred to post-v1. For v1, tasks are created only through the tasks.apply_event MCP tool.

## Notes

Parent: post-v1 / deferred. Bridges the note and task subsystems. Do not implement until the decoupled task system (brain-dhi.4) is stable.
payload.title: Implement Markdown checkbox to task creation (deferred)

payload.description:
Add an explicit vector_search_mode parameter to vector search queries, controlling the tradeoff between determinism and speed.

Modes:
- exact: No ANN index — brute-force scan over all vectors. Most deterministic, slowest. Use for golden tests and debugging.
- ann_refined: ANN candidates + deterministic rescoring on full vectors (LanceDB refine_factor). Good balance of speed and fidelity.
- ann_fast: ANN only, lowest latency but least deterministic across index rebuilds.

Implementation:
1. Add vector_search_mode to the search configuration (default: ann_refined for interactive use).
2. Pass through to LanceDB query builder: exact mode uses .without_index() or equivalent, ann_refined sets refine_factor, ann_fast uses defaults.
3. Expose as a parameter on memory.search_minimal MCP tool (optional, default from config).
4. Golden tests (brain-61e.7) should use exact mode to ensure reproducible test results.

Acceptance: (1) Golden tests using exact mode produce identical results across runs. (2) ann_refined produces results within acceptable distance of exact results. (3) Mode is configurable per-query via MCP and via config file for default.

## Notes

Parent epic: brain-61e (Phase 4). This knob enables deterministic golden tests by forcing exact vector search. Default to ann_refined for interactive use.
payload.title: Add vector_search_mode knob for determinism control

payload.description: Add defer_until: Option<i64> to TaskCreatedPayload/TaskUpdatedPayload. Modify list_ready() to exclude tasks where defer_until > now().
payload.title: Add defer_until field to task events

payload.description: Add task_type: Option<String> to TaskCreatedPayload (values: task, bug, feature, epic, chore, decision). Add task_type column to tasks projection table. Add task_type to TaskUpdatedPayload for reclassification.

payload.description: New event type: CommentAdded { comment_id: String, body: String, actor: String }. New projection table: task_comments (comment_id, task_id, body, actor, created_at).
payload.title: Add comments support to task events

payload.description:
Embed task capsules into LanceDB so tasks are searchable via the same hybrid retrieval pipeline as notes.

When a task is created or updated, generate a capsule string: '{title}. {description_summary}. Tags: {tags}. Priority: {priority}.'
Embed the capsule using the existing Candle BGE-small pipeline and store in the LanceDB chunks table with kind='task'.

This means memory.search_minimal can return both note chunks and task capsules in a single hybrid query — the agent doesn't need separate search tools for notes vs tasks.

On task completion (status_changed to done): generate an outcome capsule embedding from the task title + any completion reason. This supports 'what did we do about X?' queries.

Acceptance: (1) Tasks appear in memory.search_minimal results when semantically relevant. (2) Completed task outcomes are searchable. (3) Task embeddings are updated when task metadata changes.

## Notes

Parent epic: brain-dhi (Phase 2). Enables unified search across notes and tasks.
payload.title: Implement task capsule embedding for semantic task search

payload.description: Add 'brain tasks export markdown' CLI subcommand that exports all tasks from SQLite to individual .md files with YAML frontmatter, enabling task content to be indexed by the RAG pipeline alongside regular notes.
payload.title: brain tasks export markdown command

payload.description: In retrieval.rs:112-113 and 126-127, byte_start and byte_end are hardcoded to 0. The RankedResult struct doesn't carry byte offsets from ChunkRow, so they're lost before reaching expand_results. Fix: add byte_start/byte_end to RankedResult and populate from ChunkRow in mcp/tools.rs handle_expand.
payload.title: Pass byte offsets through to expand results

payload.description:
Add optional brain field to dependency and note-link payloads for cross-brain soft references.

SCHEMA CHANGES:
- Add Option<String> brain field to DependencyPayload (source_brain) and NoteLinkPayload (link_brain)
- Add nullable depends_on_brain column to task_deps table
- Add nullable link_brain column to task_note_links table
- NULL = local reference (zero-cost common case), non-NULL = cross-brain soft reference

RATIONALE:
Separate field (not composite brain:id string) chosen for:
- Native SQLite indexing on the brain column
- Clean JOINs without string parsing
- Efficient stale-ref queries: WHERE depends_on_brain IS NOT NULL

VALIDATION CHANGES:
- TaskStore::validate() should skip existence check for cross-brain DependencyAdded (target may not be in local SQLite)
- list_ready() JOIN should treat cross-brain deps as soft (assume satisfied unless brain doctor says otherwise)
- check_cycle() only applies to local deps (cross-brain cycles are not detectable locally)

Depends on: brain-61e.5 (registry must exist first for brain name resolution)
payload.title: Cross-brain reference support

payload.description: Generate lightweight, rule-based capsules for every chunk at ingest time to populate the summary_2sent field in search_minimal responses. Capsule formula: title (from heading hierarchy) + first meaningful sentence + heading outline. This is NOT ML summarization — it is deterministic string extraction from the parsed markdown AST. The capsule must be cheap enough to generate on every indexing pass without impacting ingest latency. ML-quality summarization is deferred to the consolidation scheduler (separate task). This is the key insight: always have a cheap capsule ready immediately, defer expensive summaries to idle time.
payload.title: Implement deterministic capsule generation on ingest

payload.description:
MCP tool for deterministic next-task selection. Returns the highest-priority ready task based on configurable policies.

Input schema:
- policy (string, optional, default 'priority'): one of 'priority', 'due_date', 'contextual'
  - priority: Sort by priority ASC, then due_ts ASC NULLS LAST, then updated_at DESC, then task_id ASC
  - due_date: Sort by due_ts ASC NULLS LAST, then priority ASC, then task_id ASC
  - contextual: Boost tasks linked to recently active notes or vault paths (requires active_context parameter)
- active_context (object, optional, for contextual policy): { recent_file_ids?: [...], recent_chunk_ids?: [...] }
- budget_tokens (integer, optional, default 400): token budget for the response
- k (integer, optional, default 1): number of tasks to return

Processing:
1. Query SQLite for ready tasks (status IN (open, in_progress) AND all dependencies done).
2. Apply policy-based sorting.
3. For contextual policy: boost tasks that have task_note_links to chunks in active_context.
4. Return top-k task capsules within budget.

Response: { results: [{ task_id, title, description_summary, status, priority, due_ts, dependency_summary: { total_deps, done_deps, blocking_tasks }, linked_notes: [{ chunk_id, file_path }], scores? }], ready_count, blocked_count }

The dependency_summary gives the agent enough context to understand why a task is ready and what it depends on without fetching the full graph.

Acceptance: (1) Returns the correct highest-priority ready task. (2) Blocked tasks are never returned. (3) Deterministic: same state produces same ordering.

## Notes

Parent epic: brain-dhi (Phase 2). Provides deterministic next-task selection for agent workflows.

payload.description: Expose TaskStore::get_task() + get_task_comments() + get_task_labels() + get_dependency_summary() via MCP. Returns full task state including comments, labels, deps, and linked notes for a single task by ID. Uses the shared task_row_to_json() from utils.rs. Agents currently cannot look up a specific task.
payload.title: Implement tasks.get MCP tool

payload.description: Expose TaskStore::list_all() / list_ready() / list_blocked() via MCP with a filter parameter for status selection. Returns task summaries (not full detail — use tasks.get for that). Agents currently can only see ready tasks via tasks.next; they cannot see blocked tasks, all tasks, or reason about the full task graph.
